options: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD); rapidly progressive dementia with characteristic MRI, EEG, and CSF findings
Alzheimer disease; start cholinesterase inhibitor
Frontotemporal dementia; behavioral variant
Lewy body dementia; start carbidopa-levodopa
questions.question: A 50-year-old male presents with progressive memory loss, personality changes, and myoclonus over 6 months. MRI shows cortical ribboning and DWI hyperintensity in the caudate and putamen. EEG shows periodic sharp wave complexes. 14-3-3 protein is elevated in CSF. What is the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Rapidly progressive dementia over weeks to months with myoclonus, MRI cortical ribboning and basal ganglia DWI/FLAIR hyperintensity, periodic sharp wave complexes on EEG, and elevated 14-3-3 protein in CSF is classic for sporadic CJD. CJD is a prion disease (misfolded PrPSc protein) that is universally fatal (median survival 5-6 months). Diagnostic criteria include progressive dementia plus 2 of: myoclonus, pyramidal/extrapyramidal signs, visual/cerebellar signs, akinetic mutism. RT-QuIC assay in CSF has greater than 95% sensitivity and specificity.

options: Fasting lipid panel at baseline and 4-12 weeks after initiation, then periodically; hepatic transaminases at baseline
Weekly creatine kinase levels for the duration of therapy
Monthly renal function tests and urinalysis
No monitoring is needed once the statin is initiated
questions.question: A 55-year-old male is initiated on a statin (atorvastatin 40 mg daily) for hyperlipidemia. What laboratory monitoring should the nurse practitioner order?
questions.rationale: Statin therapy requires a baseline lipid panel and hepatic transaminases (ALT) before initiation. A follow-up lipid panel at 4-12 weeks assesses therapeutic response. Hepatic transaminases should be rechecked if symptoms of hepatotoxicity develop. Routine CK monitoring is not recommended unless the patient develops myalgia symptoms. Renal monitoring is not a primary requirement for statin therapy. Ongoing periodic lipid panels guide treatment adequacy.

options: Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine supplementation
Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 2 months
No treatment; repeat TST in 1 year
Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 3 months only
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a positive tuberculin skin test (18 mm induration) and a normal chest X-ray has no symptoms and no known TB contacts. He was born in a country with high TB prevalence. What is the recommended treatment?
questions.rationale: Latent TB infection (positive TST, normal CXR, no symptoms) in a high-risk individual requires treatment to prevent progression to active TB. Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months is a standard regimen, with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) to prevent peripheral neuropathy. Four-drug therapy is for active TB. Observation without treatment is inappropriate in a high-risk patient. Three months of isoniazid alone is insufficient.

options: Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle to systematically test and implement workflow changes such as adjusted scheduling templates and additional support staffing
Continue current practice and document errors for annual review
Report to administration and wait for their solution
Reduce panel size to 200 patients immediately
questions.question: An NP is managing a patient panel of 1,200 patients. She notices increasing medication errors during afternoon clinic sessions. Root cause analysis reveals provider fatigue and inadequate support staff. What quality improvement framework should she apply?
questions.rationale: PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) is the standard quality improvement framework for healthcare. The NP should: Plan (identify specific changes to test), Do (implement changes on small scale), Study (measure outcomes), Act (adopt, adapt, or abandon based on results). Specific interventions might include afternoon scheduling adjustments, medication reconciliation checklists, clinical pharmacist integration, or scribing support. Simply documenting errors without intervention perpetuates harm.

options: Beta-thalassemia trait (minor); no treatment needed but genetic counseling is important for reproductive planning
Iron deficiency anemia; start iron supplementation
Alpha-thalassemia; hemoglobin H testing
Sideroblastic anemia; pyridoxine supplementation
questions.question: A 30-year-old female of Mediterranean descent presents with microcytic anemia (MCV 62, Hgb 10.2) disproportionate to the degree of anemia. Iron studies are normal. Hemoglobin electrophoresis shows HbA2 of 5.8% (normal less than 3.5%). What is the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Microcytic anemia with normal iron studies and elevated HbA2 (greater than 3.5%) is diagnostic of beta-thalassemia trait (minor). Beta-thalassemia trait is a carrier state (heterozygous beta-globin gene mutation) causing reduced beta-globin chain synthesis. Characteristic findings: mild anemia (Hgb 10-12), markedly low MCV (60-70, disproportionate to Hgb level), normal RDW (vs elevated in iron deficiency), target cells on smear, and elevated HbA2. No treatment is needed. Genetic counseling is essential: if both parents carry beta-thalassemia trait, there is a 25% chance of beta-thalassemia major (Cooley anemia) in offspring.

options: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir
Abacavir/lamivudine plus efavirenz
Zidovudine/lamivudine plus nevirapine
Start PCP prophylaxis only and defer ART until CD4 is below 100
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with HIV (CD4 count 180 cells/mm3, viral load 85,000 copies/mL) presents for initial antiretroviral therapy. He has no active opportunistic infections. HLA-B*5701 testing is negative. Hepatitis B surface antigen is positive. What is the most appropriate initial ART regimen?
questions.rationale: HIV with hepatitis B co-infection requires an ART backbone containing tenofovir plus emtricitabine (or lamivudine), which are active against both HIV and HBV. Dolutegravir is the preferred integrase inhibitor. Abacavir requires negative HLA-B*5701 (which this patient has) but does not cover HBV. Zidovudine-based regimens have more side effects. ART should never be deferred; early initiation improves outcomes. PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) is needed at CD4 below 200 but does not replace ART.

options: Long-term oxygen therapy targeting SpO2 88-92% to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance, plus diuretics for fluid overload
Right heart catheterization and pulmonary vasodilator therapy
High-dose inhaled corticosteroids
Left ventricular assist device placement
questions.question: A 60-year-old male with COPD (FEV1 45% predicted) develops cor pulmonale with right heart failure. He has peripheral edema and elevated JVP. What is the primary treatment for this complication?
questions.rationale: Cor pulmonale in COPD is primarily caused by hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction leading to pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure. Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) is the only intervention shown to reduce mortality in hypoxemic COPD by reducing pulmonary vascular resistance. Diuretics manage fluid overload. Pulmonary vasodilators used for PAH may worsen V/Q mismatch in COPD. LVAD is for left heart failure.

options: Reduce or adjust antihypertensive medications and reassess orthostatic symptoms
Add midodrine for orthostatic hypotension
Increase the antihypertensive medication dose to lower sitting blood pressure
Prescribe fludrocortisone for volume expansion
questions.question: A 77-year-old patient with a history of falls has a blood pressure of 148/82 mmHg while sitting and 120/68 mmHg upon standing. The patient reports dizziness with position changes. What adjustment should the NP consider?
questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension (drop of 20 mmHg or more in systolic BP upon standing) in an older adult on antihypertensives is a significant fall risk factor. The priority is to reduce or adjust antihypertensive medications, particularly those contributing to orthostatic symptoms. Adding midodrine or fludrocortisone without first addressing the iatrogenic cause is inappropriate. Increasing the antihypertensive dose would worsen orthostatic symptoms and fall risk.

options: Conduct a thorough safety assessment including suicidality screening (Columbia Protocol), assess for underlying psychiatric disorders, and refer for evidence-based therapy (DBT for adolescents)
Hospitalize immediately for any self-harm behavior
Prescribe an SSRI and schedule follow-up in 4 weeks
Reassure parents that cutting is a phase and will resolve
questions.question: A 16-year-old female presents with self-harm behavior (superficial cutting on forearms), emotional dysregulation, and academic decline. She denies suicidal intent. Her parents are present and concerned. What is the NP's initial approach?
questions.rationale: Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) requires careful assessment distinguishing self-harm from suicidal behavior using validated tools (Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale). While NSSI without suicidal intent may not require hospitalization, it signals emotional distress and increases suicide risk over time. DBT for adolescents (DBT-A) has the strongest evidence for reducing self-harm. Underlying conditions (depression, PTSD, BPD traits) should be evaluated. Dismissing self-harm as a phase ignores serious psychological distress.

options: Pain management with calcitonin nasal spray, limited bed rest, bracing if needed, and restart bisphosphonate therapy with calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Immediate surgical spinal fusion
High-dose oral corticosteroids for 2 weeks
Bed rest for 6 weeks followed by physical therapy
questions.question: A 70-year-old female presents with sudden onset of severe lower back pain after lifting groceries. She has a history of osteoporosis and has not been taking her prescribed alendronate. X-ray shows a compression fracture at T12. What is the acute and long-term management?
questions.rationale: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures are managed conservatively in most cases. Acute treatment includes analgesics (calcitonin nasal spray has analgesic properties specific to bone pain), short-term activity modification (not prolonged bed rest which worsens bone loss), and thoracolumbar bracing for comfort. Long-term management requires restarting bisphosphonate therapy, calcium (1200 mg/day), vitamin D (800-1000 IU/day), and fall prevention. Surgery is reserved for fractures with neurological compromise. Steroids worsen osteoporosis. Prolonged bed rest accelerates bone loss.

options: Patellofemoral pain syndrome; quadriceps strengthening (VMO focus), patellar taping, activity modification, and hip abductor strengthening
ACL tear; MRI and surgical referral
Meniscal tear; arthroscopic surgery
Patellar tendinitis; corticosteroid injection
questions.question: A 35-year-old female runner presents with progressive anterior knee pain worse with climbing stairs, prolonged sitting, and squatting. She has a positive Clarke test (patellar grind). There is no effusion or instability. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?
questions.rationale: Anterior knee pain exacerbated by activities that load the patellofemoral joint (stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting/'theater sign') with positive Clarke test is classic patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS), the most common cause of anterior knee pain. First-line management is physical therapy focusing on quadriceps strengthening (particularly vastus medialis oblique), hip abductor and external rotator strengthening, and activity modification. Patellar taping or bracing may provide symptomatic relief. ACL tears present with instability. Meniscal tears have joint line tenderness and locking.

options: Diphenhydramine due to its strong anticholinergic effects increasing delirium and cognitive decline risk
Melatonin 3 mg at bedtime
Trazodone 25-50 mg at bedtime
Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia as first-line treatment
questions.question: A 72-year-old patient with insomnia and mild cognitive impairment asks for a sleep medication. Which agent should the nurse practitioner avoid due to anticholinergic burden?
questions.rationale: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation antihistamine with strong anticholinergic properties. In elderly patients, particularly those with cognitive impairment, anticholinergic medications increase the risk of delirium, cognitive decline, falls, urinary retention, and constipation. The Beers Criteria strongly recommend avoiding anticholinergic medications in older adults. Melatonin and low-dose trazodone have more favorable safety profiles. CBT-I is the recommended first-line treatment for chronic insomnia.

options: McDonald criteria for multiple sclerosis (dissemination in space and can establish dissemination in time with follow-up or CSF findings)
Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis
Clinically isolated syndrome only
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with optic neuritis (painful vision loss in the left eye). MRI brain shows 4 periventricular white matter lesions. MRI spine shows a cervical cord lesion. Visual evoked potentials are prolonged. What criteria does this presentation meet?
questions.rationale: The 2017 McDonald criteria for MS require dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS is met by lesions in 2 or more typical CNS areas (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). This patient has periventricular and spinal cord lesions. DIT can be met by a new lesion on follow-up MRI, simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. NMOSD should be excluded with aquaporin-4 antibody testing.

options: Switch to a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) such as apixaban or rivaroxaban given poor INR control (time in therapeutic range likely below 65%)
Continue warfarin and check INR more frequently
Discontinue all anticoagulation
Add aspirin to warfarin for better protection
questions.question: A 65-year-old male with atrial fibrillation and CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 has been on warfarin for 5 years. His last 3 INR readings have been 1.6, 3.8, and 1.9 (erratic control). What is the recommended action?
questions.rationale: Poor INR control (time in therapeutic range below 65%) on warfarin is associated with increased stroke and bleeding risk compared to stable INR or DOAC therapy. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) provide predictable anticoagulation without routine monitoring, have fewer drug-food interactions, and have demonstrated non-inferiority or superiority to warfarin for stroke prevention in AF (RE-LY, ROCKET AF, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48 trials). Apixaban (ARISTOTLE) showed the lowest major bleeding rate. Switching is recommended unless the patient has a mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis.

options: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies
Start a gluten-free diet immediately without biopsy
Colonoscopy with random biopsies
Hydrogen breath test for lactose intolerance
questions.question: A 42-year-old male presents with chronic watery diarrhea, bloating, and iron deficiency anemia. He reports no weight loss. Anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) IgA is strongly positive. Total IgA level is normal. What is the next diagnostic step?
questions.rationale: Strongly positive anti-tTG IgA with normal total IgA is highly suggestive of celiac disease, but histologic confirmation with duodenal biopsy (showing villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis) is required before initiating a lifelong gluten-free diet. Starting the diet before biopsy can cause histologic normalization, making diagnosis impossible. Colonoscopy does not evaluate the duodenum. Hydrogen breath test is for carbohydrate malabsorption.

options: Antiarrhythmics that prolong QT (sotalol, amiodarone), macrolide antibiotics, fluoroquinolones, and certain antipsychotics
Beta-blockers
Calcium channel blockers
ACE inhibitors
questions.question: A 45-year-old female is diagnosed with long QT syndrome after an episode of torsades de pointes. She has a corrected QT interval of 520 ms on ECG. What medications must she strictly avoid?
questions.rationale: Long QT syndrome patients must avoid all QT-prolonging medications to prevent torsades de pointes. These include class III antiarrhythmics (sotalol, dofetilide), macrolides (azithromycin, erythromycin), fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin), antipsychotics (haloperidol, ziprasidone), and many others. Beta-blockers are actually first-line treatment for congenital LQTS. The Arizona CERT QT drugs list should be referenced.

options: Upper airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip), asthma (cough-variant), and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
Lung cancer, tuberculosis, and pneumonia
Heart failure, pulmonary embolism, and COPD
Psychogenic cough, vocal cord dysfunction, and anxiety
questions.question: A 45-year-old female presents with chronic cough for 8 weeks. She is a non-smoker, CXR is normal, and she is not on ACE inhibitors. She has postnasal drip and throat clearing. What are the three most common causes of chronic cough in adults with a normal CXR?
questions.rationale: The diagnostic triad of chronic cough (lasting 8 or more weeks) in non-smoking adults with a normal CXR and not on ACE inhibitors: 1) Upper airway cough syndrome/UACS (formerly postnasal drip syndrome) -- treat empirically with first-generation antihistamine/decongestant, 2) Cough-variant asthma -- diagnose with methacholine challenge or empiric ICS trial, 3) GERD (may be 'silent' without heartburn) -- empiric PPI trial for 8 weeks. These three conditions account for 90%+ of chronic cough cases. Systematic evaluation following the ACCP guidelines algorithm resolves most cases.

options: Nodular basal cell carcinoma; Mohs micrographic surgery for facial lesion
Squamous cell carcinoma; wide local excision
Sebaceous cyst; observation
Keratoacanthoma; shave removal
questions.question: A 65-year-old male presents with a pearly, translucent papule with telangiectasias and a central depression on the nose. It has been slowly growing over 8 months. There are no other lesions. What is the most likely diagnosis and recommended treatment?
questions.rationale: A pearly, translucent papule with telangiectasias and central depression (rolled borders with pearly edge) is the classic description of nodular basal cell carcinoma, the most common skin cancer. Mohs micrographic surgery is preferred for facial BCC (especially nose) as it provides the highest cure rate (99%) with maximal tissue conservation. Standard excision with 4-5 mm margins is appropriate for trunk/extremity BCC. BCC rarely metastasizes but can cause significant local tissue destruction if untreated.

options: Rocky Mountain spotted fever; doxycycline 100 mg BID immediately
Lyme disease; amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 21 days
Measles; supportive care and isolation
Drug reaction; discontinue all medications
questions.question: A 32-year-old female presents with a 4-day history of fever, headache, myalgias, and a maculopapular rash that began on the wrists and ankles and spread centrally. She went hiking in a wooded area 1 week ago. What is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?
questions.rationale: Centripetal rash (starting on extremities and spreading centrally) with fever, headache, and myalgias after outdoor exposure in an endemic area is classic for Rocky Mountain spotted fever (Rickettsia rickettsii). Doxycycline is the treatment of choice and must be started empirically without waiting for serologic confirmation, as delays increase mortality. Lyme disease presents with erythema migrans, not centripetal rash. Measles rash starts on the face. Drug reactions have different distribution patterns.

options: CT cervical spine
Plain radiograph of the cervical spine (3-view)
MRI of the cervical spine
No imaging if Canadian C-Spine Rule criteria are met for clearance
questions.question: A 25-year-old patient presents after a motor vehicle collision with neck pain and midline cervical tenderness. The patient is alert, oriented, and neurologically intact. Which imaging study should the NP order?
questions.rationale: Midline cervical tenderness after trauma is a positive finding on the Canadian C-Spine Rule and indicates the need for imaging. CT cervical spine is the preferred modality in trauma settings due to superior sensitivity for fracture detection compared to plain radiographs. MRI is used to evaluate ligamentous injury and spinal cord pathology but is not the first-line study. The Canadian C-Spine Rule cannot clear this patient because midline tenderness is present.

options: Reduce the maximum daily dose to no more than 2000 mg per day
Continue the current dose as hepatic impairment does not affect acetaminophen dosing
Increase to 1500 mg every 6 hours for improved pain control
Switch to ibuprofen 600 mg every 8 hours instead
questions.question: A 68-year-old female with moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh class B) requires pain management. She has been taking acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours. What prescribing change should the nurse practitioner make?
questions.rationale: In patients with hepatic impairment, the maximum daily dose of acetaminophen should be reduced to 2000 mg per day or less to minimize the risk of hepatotoxicity. The liver metabolizes acetaminophen, and impaired hepatic function increases the accumulation of the toxic metabolite NAPQI. Continuing the full dose risks liver damage. NSAIDs are also problematic in liver disease due to bleeding risk from coagulopathy and potential renal effects.

options: Cauda equina syndrome; emergent MRI of the lumbar spine and urgent surgical consultation for decompression within 24-48 hours
Lumbar disc herniation; prescribe NSAIDs and physical therapy
Spinal stenosis; elective MRI in 2 weeks
Sciatica; prescribe gabapentin and follow up in 1 month
questions.question: A 35-year-old male presents with acute low back pain and bilateral lower extremity weakness, urinary retention, and saddle anesthesia. What is the diagnosis and immediate management?
questions.rationale: Bilateral lower extremity weakness, urinary retention, and saddle anesthesia constitute cauda equina syndrome -- a surgical emergency. Delayed decompression (beyond 24-48 hours) risks permanent neurological deficits including paralysis and bladder/bowel dysfunction. Emergent MRI confirms the diagnosis. Conservative management is inappropriate and delays critical intervention.

options: Evidence-based practice model: formulate a PICO question, systematically search the literature, critically appraise the evidence, implement the change with stakeholder engagement, and evaluate outcomes
Implement the change immediately based on personal clinical experience
Wait for a national mandate before changing practice
Ask pharmaceutical representatives for guidance on pain management protocols
questions.question: An NP is considering implementing a new evidence-based protocol for managing chronic pain in her practice. The protocol would shift from opioid-first to multimodal pain management. What process should guide this practice change?
questions.rationale: Evidence-based practice implementation follows a structured approach: 1) Formulate a PICO question (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome), 2) Conduct a systematic literature search, 3) Critically appraise evidence quality (using tools like the Johns Hopkins EBP model), 4) Engage stakeholders (patients, providers, administrators) for buy-in, 5) Implement using a pilot or phased approach, 6) Evaluate outcomes and adjust. Clinical practice changes should be guided by the best available evidence combined with clinical expertise and patient preferences -- not personal opinion or industry influence.

options: Continue dual antiplatelet therapy for a total of 12 months post-STEMI
Discontinue ticagrelor now since LDL is at target
Switch to clopidogrel for indefinite use
Stop both ASA and ticagrelor and continue statin only
questions.question: A 70-year-old male with a history of STEMI 3 months ago presents for follow-up. Current medications include ASA, ticagrelor, metoprolol, ramipril, and rosuvastatin. His LDL is 1.4 mmol/L and BP is 118/72. He asks about stopping ticagrelor. What is the appropriate recommendation?
questions.rationale: Post-STEMI, dual antiplatelet therapy (ASA plus a P2Y12 inhibitor such as ticagrelor) should be continued for 12 months to reduce the risk of stent thrombosis and recurrent events. Discontinuing at 3 months is premature. Switching to clopidogrel indefinitely is not standard. Stopping both antiplatelet agents dramatically increases thrombotic risk.

options: CT chest with contrast followed by CT-guided biopsy or bronchoscopy
Repeat chest X-ray in 3 months for interval change
Sputum cytology and acid-fast bacilli smear
Start empiric antibiotics for possible pneumonia
questions.question: A 65-year-old female presents with a 3-week history of cough with blood-streaked sputum, unintentional weight loss of 5 kg, and a 30 pack-year smoking history. Chest X-ray shows a 3.5 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
questions.rationale: A spiculated lung mass in a long-term smoker with hemoptysis and weight loss is highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy. CT chest with contrast provides detailed staging information, and tissue biopsy (CT-guided or bronchoscopic) is essential for histologic diagnosis. Watchful waiting with repeat imaging risks delaying diagnosis. Sputum cytology has low sensitivity. Empiric antibiotics delay workup of a likely malignancy.

options: Omeprazole 20 mg daily for 4-8 weeks
Ranitidine 150 mg twice daily indefinitely
Sucralfate 1 g four times daily
Metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with a history of GERD presents with persistent heartburn despite lifestyle modifications. He has no alarm symptoms. Which medication should the nurse practitioner prescribe as first-line therapy?
questions.rationale: Proton pump inhibitors such as omeprazole are first-line pharmacotherapy for GERD when lifestyle modifications are insufficient. They provide superior acid suppression compared to H2 receptor antagonists. A 4-8 week course is standard initial therapy. H2 blockers are second-line. Sucralfate is used primarily for peptic ulcer disease. Metoclopramide is a prokinetic agent with significant adverse effects and is not first-line for GERD.

options: CT-guided biopsy or referral for surgical resection
Repeat CT in 3 months for interval growth assessment
Prescribe empiric antibiotics for 2 weeks and repeat imaging
PET scan in 6 months
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a 3 cm solitary pulmonary nodule on CT has a PET scan showing SUV of 8.5. He is a current smoker with a 30-pack-year history. What is the recommended next step?
questions.rationale: A large (greater than 3 cm), PET-avid (SUV greater than 2.5) pulmonary nodule in a heavy smoker has a very high probability of malignancy. Tissue diagnosis via CT-guided biopsy or surgical resection is indicated. Watchful waiting with serial imaging is inappropriate for high-risk lesions. Empiric antibiotics delay diagnosis. Repeat PET adds no diagnostic value.

options: Hypertensive emergency with target organ damage; admit for IV antihypertensive therapy with controlled BP reduction of 25% in first hour
Hypertensive urgency; prescribe oral amlodipine and follow up in 1 week
Essential hypertension; start lisinopril 10 mg daily
White coat hypertension; ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
questions.question: A 35-year-old male with no significant history presents with blood pressure of 185/115 mmHg. He has grade III retinopathy, serum creatinine of 2.4 (baseline 0.9), and proteinuria on dipstick. What is this clinical presentation and initial management?
questions.rationale: Severely elevated BP with evidence of target organ damage (grade III retinopathy, acute kidney injury, proteinuria) constitutes a hypertensive emergency requiring admission and IV antihypertensive therapy. BP should be reduced by no more than 25% in the first hour to prevent ischemic complications, then gradually to 160/100 over the next 2-6 hours. Oral therapy alone is insufficient. This is not urgency (no organ damage) or white coat hypertension.

options: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase, blocking azathioprine metabolism and causing life-threatening myelosuppression
Allopurinol increases azathioprine absorption requiring a formulation change
The combination has no clinically significant interaction
Allopurinol enhances azathioprine metabolism requiring dose increases
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is managing a patient on azathioprine for inflammatory bowel disease. The patient is prescribed allopurinol for concurrent gout. What critical interaction must be addressed?
questions.rationale: Azathioprine is metabolized to 6-mercaptopurine, which is inactivated by xanthine oxidase. Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase, causing dramatic accumulation of 6-mercaptopurine and severe, potentially fatal bone marrow suppression. If co-administration is absolutely necessary, the azathioprine dose must be reduced by 75% and CBC monitored very frequently. This is one of the most critical drug interactions in clinical practice.

options: Plaque psoriasis; first-line for mild disease is topical corticosteroids (medium-high potency for body, low potency for face/folds) and vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene)
Eczema; moisturizers only
Tinea corporis; topical antifungals
Seborrheic dermatitis; ketoconazole shampoo
questions.question: A 35-year-old male presents with well-demarcated, silvery-white scaly plaques on his elbows, knees, and scalp. He has pitting of his fingernails. What is the diagnosis and initial management for mild disease?
questions.rationale: Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp with nail pitting is classic plaque psoriasis. Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed) is characteristic. Management is stepwise by severity: Mild (less than 3% BSA): topical corticosteroids (betamethasone, clobetasol for thick plaques; hydrocortisone for face/folds), vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene), or combination products. Moderate-severe: phototherapy (narrowband UVB), conventional systemics (methotrexate, cyclosporine, acitretin), or biologic therapy. Nail psoriasis often requires systemic therapy.

options: Add a biologic DMARD (TNF inhibitor such as adalimumab or etanercept) or switch to a JAK inhibitor
Increase methotrexate to 30 mg weekly
Add hydroxychloroquine only
Switch to NSAIDs as primary therapy
questions.question: A 60-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 20 mg weekly has persistent synovitis and elevated CRP despite 3 months of therapy. DAS28 score indicates moderate disease activity. What is the next therapeutic step?
questions.rationale: Persistent moderate-to-high disease activity despite adequate methotrexate trial (3-6 months at optimal dose) is an indication for biologic therapy. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) are first-line biologics for RA. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib) are oral alternatives. Methotrexate dose above 25 mg is rarely more effective and increases toxicity. Hydroxychloroquine alone is insufficient add-on. NSAIDs do not modify disease progression.

options: Repeat ultrasound in 8-12 weeks to assess for resolution
Immediate laparoscopic cystectomy
Start combined oral contraceptive pills to resolve the cyst
CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast for staging
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with a 2 cm right adnexal mass found on routine pelvic ultrasound. She is asymptomatic and has regular menstrual cycles. The mass appears to be a simple cyst with thin walls, no septations, and no solid components. CA-125 is normal. What is the recommended management?
questions.rationale: Simple ovarian cysts less than 5 cm in premenopausal women are almost always functional (follicular or corpus luteum) and typically resolve spontaneously within 1-3 menstrual cycles. Repeat ultrasound in 8-12 weeks confirms resolution. Surgical intervention is unnecessary for small simple cysts. OCPs prevent new cyst formation but do not hasten resolution of existing cysts. CT imaging and staging are for suspected malignancy, which is unlikely with a small simple cyst and normal CA-125.

options: Unintentional gluten exposure from hidden sources (medications, processed foods, cross-contamination); referral to a celiac-specialist dietitian
Switch to a dairy-free diet
Start systemic corticosteroids
Diagnose refractory celiac disease immediately
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with celiac disease on a strict gluten-free diet for 2 years continues to have symptoms (bloating, diarrhea, weight loss). tTG-IgA remains elevated at 45 U/mL. What should the NP investigate?
questions.rationale: Persistent symptoms and elevated tTG-IgA in celiac disease despite a 'strict' gluten-free diet is most commonly due to unintentional gluten exposure (70-90% of cases). Hidden sources include: medications/supplements (gluten-containing excipients), processed foods (soy sauce, modified food starch, malt flavoring), restaurant meals, cross-contamination during food preparation, and communion wafers. Referral to a celiac-specialist dietitian for detailed dietary review is the first step. Refractory celiac disease (true non-response to GFD) is rare and requires duodenal biopsy with immunophenotyping to diagnose.

options: Migraine with aura is associated with a 2-fold increased risk of ischemic stroke; avoid combined estrogen-containing contraceptives and tobacco; consider vascular risk factor optimization
Migraine has no cardiovascular implications
Only cluster headache increases stroke risk
Aura is a benign visual phenomenon without clinical significance
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a history of migraine with aura (visual scintillating scotoma) asks about cardiovascular risk. What should the NP counsel?
questions.rationale: Migraine with aura is an independent risk factor for ischemic stroke (2x risk increase), particularly in women under 45 who smoke and use combined oral contraceptives (risk increases to 8-10x). The aura itself involves cortical spreading depression, which may activate prothrombotic pathways. Management implications: avoid combined estrogen-containing contraceptives (progestin-only or non-hormonal methods are safe), aggressive smoking cessation, and optimize modifiable vascular risk factors. Triptans are NOT contraindicated in migraine with aura.

options: Remove the pump infusion set, give a correction dose via insulin pen or syringe, change the infusion site, and check for pump malfunction
Increase the pump basal rate and continue monitoring
Drink water and exercise to lower glucose
Continue bolusing through the pump every hour
questions.question: A 35-year-old female with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with glucose of 450 mg/dL and moderate ketones despite multiple correction boluses. Her pump site was changed 6 hours ago. What should the NP advise?
questions.rationale: Persistent hyperglycemia with ketones in a pump user suggests infusion site failure (kinked cannula, dislodged site, absorption issue). The immediate action is to bypass the pump entirely and give a correction dose via pen/syringe injection. Then change the infusion site and check the pump for malfunction. Continuing to bolus through a potentially failed site delays treatment and risks DKA progression.

options: CBC, CMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, urinalysis, and brain MRI without contrast
Brain biopsy for definitive diagnosis
PET scan as initial imaging study
Start donepezil immediately without further workup
questions.question: A 65-year-old male presents with his wife who reports he has had progressive memory loss, executive function decline, and behavioral changes over 2 years. He gets lost driving to familiar locations. MMSE score is 19/30. What initial workup should the NP order?
questions.rationale: Dementia evaluation requires ruling out reversible causes: hypothyroidism (TSH), B12 deficiency, neurosyphilis (RPR), metabolic derangements (CMP), infection (UA), and structural pathology (brain MRI). Brain biopsy is not indicated for routine dementia evaluation. PET scan may be useful adjunctively but is not the initial study. Cholinesterase inhibitors should be started after completing the diagnostic workup.

options: Assess inhaler technique and adherence; if adequate, step up to medium-dose ICS/formoterol MART
Switch to a LABA-only inhaler
Add oral montelukast without changing current therapy
Prescribe a short course of oral prednisone
questions.question: A 30-year-old female with asthma has an FEV1 of 95% predicted but reports cough predominantly at night. She uses a low-dose ICS/formoterol combination inhaler as both maintenance and reliever (MART therapy). She notes increased reliever use to 6 times in the past week. What action should the NP take?
questions.rationale: Increased reliever use (more than 2 times/week) indicates worsening asthma control. With MART (maintenance and reliever therapy), increasing reliever use signals the need to step up maintenance. First assess inhaler technique and adherence (the most common causes of poor control). If technique is correct, step up to medium-dose ICS/formoterol. LABA-only therapy is never appropriate. Montelukast alone is unlikely to provide sufficient control. Oral steroids are for exacerbations.

options: Aromatase inhibitor-associated musculoskeletal syndrome, a known class effect occurring in up to 50% of patients
An unrelated rheumatologic condition requiring referral without considering medication effects
A sign of disease progression requiring immediate oncology referral
An allergic reaction to letrozole requiring immediate discontinuation
questions.question: A 55-year-old female on long-term aromatase inhibitor therapy (letrozole) for breast cancer reports progressive joint pain and stiffness. What is the nurse practitioner's assessment of these symptoms?
questions.rationale: Aromatase inhibitor-associated musculoskeletal syndrome (AIMSS) is a well-recognized adverse effect of aromatase inhibitors, affecting up to 50% of patients. It manifests as arthralgia, joint stiffness, myalgia, and reduced grip strength, typically within the first few months of therapy. It is caused by estrogen deprivation effects on joints and tendons. Management includes exercise, NSAIDs, vitamin D supplementation, or switching to a different aromatase inhibitor. It is not an allergy or disease progression.

options: Myelopathy (spinal cord injury/edema) from chronic compression; this is an indication for surgical decompression
Normal aging finding; no intervention needed
Multiple sclerosis plaque; start disease-modifying therapy
Disc herniation only; conservative management
questions.question: A 60-year-old female with a history of falls presents with bilateral hand weakness, difficulty with fine motor tasks, and Lhermitte sign (electric shock sensation down the spine with neck flexion). MRI cervical spine shows multilevel cervical spondylosis with cord compression and T2 hyperintensity within the cord. What does the T2 hyperintensity represent?
questions.rationale: T2 hyperintensity within the cervical spinal cord in the setting of cervical spondylotic stenosis represents myelopathy (cord edema, gliosis, or demyelination from chronic compression). This is a surgical indication because myelopathy typically progresses without decompression. Lhermitte sign (also seen in MS) indicates posterior column involvement. Surgical options include anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) for 1-2 levels or posterior laminoplasty for multilevel disease. Outcomes are better with earlier intervention before significant cord damage occurs.

options: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis (bird fancier lung); antigen avoidance is the primary treatment
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis; start nintedanib
Sarcoidosis; start prednisone
Lung adenocarcinoma; refer for biopsy
questions.question: A 55-year-old female presents with progressive dyspnea and dry cough. CT chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacities with a mosaic attenuation pattern. She has pet birds at home. Serum precipitins to avian antigens are positive. What is the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from avian antigen exposure (bird fancier lung/pigeon breeder disease) presents with insidious dyspnea and cough in chronic form. CT findings include mosaic attenuation (air trapping), ground-glass opacities, and fibrosis in chronic disease. Serum precipitins (IgG antibodies to avian proteins) support the diagnosis. Primary treatment is complete antigen avoidance, which can halt or reverse early disease. Chronic fibrotic HP may require corticosteroids and has a prognosis similar to IPF if exposure continues.

options: Type A aortic dissection
Acute myocardial infarction
Pulmonary embolism
Tension pneumothorax
questions.question: A 35-year-old female with no cardiac history presents with acute onset severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back. BP is 200/110 mmHg in the right arm and 160/90 mmHg in the left arm. CXR shows widened mediastinum. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Sudden severe tearing chest/back pain, blood pressure differential between arms (greater than 20 mmHg systolic), and widened mediastinum on CXR constitute the classic triad for aortic dissection. Type A involves the ascending aorta and is a surgical emergency. Immediate management includes IV esmolol (target HR less than 60, SBP less than 120) BEFORE vasodilators, and urgent CT angiography or TEE for confirmation. Thrombolytics and anticoagulation are contraindicated. Mortality increases 1-2% per hour without surgery.

options: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) or benralizumab (anti-IL-5 receptor alpha)
Omalizumab (anti-IgE)
Rituximab (anti-CD20)
Infliximab (anti-TNF)
questions.question: A 40-year-old male with severe persistent asthma is on high-dose ICS/LABA and has been requiring oral prednisone 20 mg daily for 8 months. Eosinophil count is 600 cells/mcL. Which biologic targets eosinophilic inflammation and can reduce oral steroid dependence?
questions.rationale: Anti-IL-5 biologics (mepolizumab, reslizumab) and anti-IL-5R (benralizumab) specifically target eosinophilic inflammation and have demonstrated significant oral corticosteroid-sparing effect in severe eosinophilic asthma. Benralizumab depletes eosinophils via ADCC and showed 75% median steroid dose reduction in the ZONDA trial. Mepolizumab reduced steroid dose by 50% in the SIRIUS trial. These biologics are indicated for severe asthma with eosinophils 150-300+ cells/mcL depending on the specific agent.

options: Annual low-dose CT scan; offer smoking cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy concurrently
Chest X-ray annually
No screening until symptomatic
Sputum cytology every 6 months
questions.question: A 65-year-old female who has smoked 1 pack per day for 35 years asks about lung cancer screening. She currently smokes. What is the USPSTF recommendation?
questions.rationale: USPSTF recommends annual low-dose CT (LDCT) for adults aged 50-80 with a 20-pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years. This patient qualifies with 35 pack-years of active smoking. Screening should be combined with smoking cessation intervention. Chest X-ray lacks sensitivity for early lung cancer detection. Sputum cytology is not recommended for screening.

options: Bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI, bismuth subsalicylate, metronidazole, tetracycline) for 14 days or clarithromycin-based triple therapy for 14 days if local clarithromycin resistance is less than 15%
Single antibiotic for 7 days
PPI monotherapy for 8 weeks
Test of cure is not needed after treatment
questions.question: A 50-year-old male presents with epigastric pain relieved by eating and worsened 2-3 hours after meals. He tests positive for Helicobacter pylori on urea breath test. He has no alarm symptoms. What is the recommended eradication regimen?
questions.rationale: H. pylori eradication is recommended for all infected patients. ACG 2017 guidelines recommend 14-day treatment duration (superior to 7-10 day). In areas with greater than 15% clarithromycin resistance (most of the US), bismuth quadruple therapy is preferred: PPI BID + bismuth subsalicylate QID + metronidazole TID + tetracycline QID. If clarithromycin resistance is less than 15%, triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) for 14 days is acceptable. Test of cure is recommended 4 or more weeks after completing therapy using urea breath test or stool antigen (NOT serology). Confirm eradication in all peptic ulcer cases.

options: Refer for surgical consultation for diagnostic thyroid lobectomy
Repeat FNA in 6 months
Start levothyroxine suppression therapy
Order radioactive iodine ablation
questions.question: A 45-year-old male presents with a 2 cm thyroid nodule found incidentally on CT scan. TSH is normal. Fine needle aspiration cytology shows Bethesda category IV (follicular neoplasm). What is the appropriate next step?
questions.rationale: Bethesda category IV (follicular neoplasm or suspicious for follicular neoplasm) cannot distinguish between benign follicular adenoma and follicular carcinoma on cytology alone. Diagnostic surgical lobectomy is recommended for definitive diagnosis. Repeating FNA will yield the same indeterminate result. TSH suppression therapy is not indicated for diagnostic purposes. Radioactive iodine ablation is a treatment modality, not a diagnostic step.

options: Central vertigo (likely posterior circulation stroke); emergent neuroimaging and neurology consultation
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo; perform Epley maneuver
Vestibular neuritis; prescribe meclizine and follow up in 1 week
Meniere disease; restrict sodium and prescribe a diuretic
questions.question: A 78-year-old male presents with sudden onset vertigo, nausea, and gait ataxia. He cannot walk without assistance. HINTS exam shows direction-changing nystagmus and a normal head impulse test. What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate action?
questions.rationale: HINTS exam findings of direction-changing nystagmus and normal head impulse test are concerning for central vertigo (posterior circulation stroke). These are the opposite of peripheral findings (unidirectional nystagmus, abnormal head impulse test). In an elderly patient with vascular risk factors, emergent neuroimaging is critical. BPPV has positional nystagmus. Vestibular neuritis has a positive head impulse test.

options: Electrical storm; administer IV amiodarone, beta-blocker optimization, and consider catheter ablation
Normal ICD function; no intervention needed
ICD malfunction; schedule device replacement
Electromagnetic interference; remove external sources
questions.question: A 58-year-old male with heart failure and LVEF 25% has an ICD. He receives 3 appropriate shocks in one hour for sustained ventricular tachycardia. He is hemodynamically stable between episodes. What is this called and what is the management?
questions.rationale: Electrical storm is defined as 3 or more sustained VT/VF episodes or appropriate ICD shocks within 24 hours. Management: IV amiodarone (loading dose then infusion) to suppress arrhythmia, optimize beta-blockade (IV esmolol if needed), correct electrolytes, and anxiolysis. If refractory, catheter ablation is indicated. Repeated ICD shocks are traumatic and increase mortality. VT storm may indicate worsening heart failure, ischemia, or electrolyte disturbance that must be identified and treated.

options: Excisional lymph node biopsy
Fine needle aspiration of the lymph node
CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Monospot test for infectious mononucleosis
questions.question: A 55-year-old patient presents with painless cervical lymphadenopathy, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 8 kg over 3 months. CBC shows normal values. What is the most important next diagnostic step?
questions.rationale: Painless lymphadenopathy with B symptoms (night sweats, weight loss greater than 10% of body weight) raises strong suspicion for lymphoma. Excisional lymph node biopsy is the gold standard as it preserves tissue architecture needed for accurate histologic classification and subtyping. Fine needle aspiration may be insufficient for lymphoma diagnosis. CT staging is important but follows tissue diagnosis. Monospot testing is appropriate for younger patients with acute symptoms suggestive of EBV.

options: Decline to provide results to the parent, citing the minor consent statute that protects confidentiality for STI-related services
Provide the results since the parent is the legal guardian
Ask the teen for permission before releasing results
Release only negative results but withhold positive findings
questions.question: A 16-year-old presents requesting STI testing and treatment without parental involvement. The state allows minor consent for STI services. The teen's parent calls requesting test results. What should the NP do?
questions.rationale: Most states have minor consent laws allowing adolescents to consent for STI testing and treatment without parental involvement, which includes confidentiality protections. The NP must uphold these protections regardless of parental request. Guardian status does not override minor consent statutes for STI services. Selectively releasing results violates the confidentiality principle.

options: Serotonin syndrome
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Malignant hyperthermia
Anticholinergic toxicity
questions.question: A 70-year-old female with depression and chronic pain is started on duloxetine 30 mg daily. She also takes tramadol PRN for pain. Two weeks later, she presents with agitation, hyperthermia (39.1 degrees Celsius), myoclonus, hyperreflexia, and diarrhea. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Serotonin syndrome results from excess serotonergic activity, commonly from combining serotonergic agents. Duloxetine (SNRI) and tramadol (weak serotonin reuptake inhibitor and mu-opioid agonist) together increase serotonin syndrome risk. Clinical triad includes altered mental status, autonomic instability (hyperthermia, tachycardia, diarrhea), and neuromuscular hyperactivity (myoclonus, hyperreflexia, clonus). NMS is associated with dopamine antagonists and presents with lead-pipe rigidity. Malignant hyperthermia occurs with inhalational anesthetics. Anticholinergic toxicity causes dry skin, urinary retention, and mydriasis.

options: Methacholine challenge test
High-resolution CT of the chest
Arterial blood gas analysis
Peak expiratory flow rate at rest
questions.question: A 40-year-old non-smoking female presents with episodic wheezing and chest tightness. Baseline spirometry is normal. Which test should the NP order to confirm the diagnosis of asthma?
questions.rationale: When baseline spirometry is normal in a patient with suspected asthma, a bronchial provocation test such as methacholine challenge can demonstrate airway hyperresponsiveness. A positive test (20% decline in FEV1 at a threshold concentration) supports the diagnosis. CT chest evaluates parenchymal disease but does not diagnose asthma. ABG is non-specific. A single peak flow measurement at rest does not assess variability or hyperresponsiveness.

options: Polypharmacy with fall risk requiring comprehensive medication review using the Beers Criteria to identify potentially inappropriate medications
The MMSE score suggesting early dementia requiring cholinesterase inhibitor initiation
Social isolation requiring referral to community programs
Nutritional assessment for potential malnutrition
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is performing a comprehensive geriatric assessment on an 80-year-old female. She scores 22/30 on the Mini-Mental State Exam (MMSE), reports two falls in the past 3 months, and takes 12 medications. Which concern requires the most immediate intervention?
questions.rationale: Polypharmacy (5 or more medications) combined with recurrent falls is the most immediately actionable finding because medications are the most modifiable fall risk factor. The Beers Criteria identifies potentially inappropriate medications in older adults (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids, certain antihypertensives) that increase fall risk. Deprescribing can have immediate safety benefits. The MMSE score (22/30) suggests mild cognitive impairment warranting further evaluation but is less immediately dangerous. Social isolation and nutrition are important but less urgent than fall prevention.

options: Cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil 5 mg daily, titrated to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks)
Memantine 10 mg twice daily
Combination donepezil and memantine simultaneously
No pharmacological treatment until MMSE drops below 15
questions.question: A 65-year-old male with newly diagnosed Alzheimer dementia (MMSE 20/30) presents with his family. His medications include atorvastatin and lisinopril. What pharmacological treatment should be initiated?
questions.rationale: Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) are first-line for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease. Donepezil is started at 5 mg daily and titrated to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks. Memantine (NMDA receptor antagonist) is indicated for moderate-to-severe dementia and may be added later. Combination therapy from the start is not standard for mild disease. Waiting to treat delays potential cognitive benefit.

options: HLA-B*1502 allele testing to assess risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome
CYP2D6 genotyping for dose adjustment
BRCA1 testing for cancer risk assessment
Factor V Leiden testing for thrombotic risk
questions.question: A 60-year-old male is started on carbamazepine for trigeminal neuralgia. Which genetic test should ideally be performed before initiating therapy in patients of Southeast Asian descent?
questions.rationale: HLA-B*1502 is strongly associated with carbamazepine-induced Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, particularly in patients of Southeast Asian, South Asian, and East Asian descent. FDA labeling recommends HLA-B*1502 testing before initiating carbamazepine in at-risk populations. Patients who test positive should not receive carbamazepine. CYP2D6, BRCA1, and Factor V Leiden testing are not relevant to carbamazepine prescribing.

options: Joint aspiration showing negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy; treat with colchicine, NSAIDs, or corticosteroids
Serum uric acid level is sufficient for diagnosis
X-ray of the foot
Blood culture to rule out septic arthritis
questions.question: A 55-year-old male presents with acute onset of severe right first metatarsophalangeal joint pain, redness, and swelling. The pain started overnight and he cannot bear weight. He ate a large steak dinner and drank several beers the night before. Serum uric acid is 9.2 mg/dL. What is the definitive diagnostic test and acute treatment?
questions.rationale: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis showing negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate (MSU) crystals under compensated polarized light microscopy is the gold standard for gout diagnosis. Serum uric acid can be normal during an acute flare (negative acute-phase reactant effect) and is unreliable for diagnosis. Acute treatment: colchicine (most effective within 24 hours of onset), NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen), or corticosteroids (oral or intra-articular). Do NOT start allopurinol during an acute flare (can worsen symptoms). Triggers include purine-rich foods, alcohol (especially beer), and dehydration.

options: Beers Criteria
Braden Scale
Norton Scale
Morse Fall Scale
questions.question: An NP is performing a comprehensive geriatric assessment on an 82-year-old patient taking 12 medications. Which validated tool should be used to assess for potentially inappropriate medications?
questions.rationale: The Beers Criteria (American Geriatrics Society) is the standard tool for identifying potentially inappropriate medications (PIMs) in older adults. It lists medications that should be avoided or used with caution due to age-related changes in pharmacokinetics and increased sensitivity. The Braden Scale assesses pressure injury risk. The Norton Scale also assesses pressure ulcer risk. The Morse Fall Scale assesses fall risk.

options: Within 1-2 weeks of starting therapy and after dose changes
Only at the annual physical examination
Six months after starting therapy
No monitoring is needed for ACE inhibitors
questions.question: A nurse practitioner starts a patient on an ACE inhibitor for hypertension. When should serum potassium and creatinine be rechecked after initiation?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors can cause hyperkalemia by reducing aldosterone secretion and can impair renal function, especially in patients with underlying renal artery stenosis or chronic kidney disease. Serum potassium and creatinine should be checked within 1-2 weeks of initiation and after each dose adjustment. A rise in creatinine greater than 30% from baseline warrants evaluation and possible discontinuation. Annual monitoring alone is insufficient to detect early adverse effects.

options: CBD inhibits CYP2C19, increasing levels of norclobazam (active metabolite) and sedation risk
CBD enhances clobazam metabolism, requiring higher clobazam doses
There is no interaction between CBD and clobazam
CBD converts clobazam into a nephrotoxic metabolite
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is evaluating a patient using CBD oil who is also taking clobazam for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. What interaction should be monitored?
questions.rationale: CBD is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C19, which is responsible for metabolizing clobazam to its active metabolite norclobazam, and for further metabolism of norclobazam. CBD inhibition leads to increased norclobazam levels, potentiating sedation, somnolence, and other CNS depressant effects. This interaction was identified during clinical trials of Epidiolex (pharmaceutical-grade CBD) for seizure disorders. Clobazam dose reduction may be necessary when CBD is co-administered.

options: Competitive high-intensity sports such as basketball
Low-intensity walking programs
Supervised cardiac rehabilitation
Gentle yoga and stretching
questions.question: A patient with known hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) asks about exercise recommendations. Which activity should the NP advise the patient to avoid?
questions.rationale: Patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy are at increased risk of sudden cardiac death during high-intensity competitive sports due to dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction and arrhythmias. Current guidelines recommend avoiding competitive high-intensity exercise. Low-intensity walking, supervised rehabilitation, and gentle yoga are generally considered safe with appropriate medical clearance.

options: Time in range greater than 70%, time below range less than 4%, time below 54 mg/dL less than 1%, and GMI/estimated A1c less than 7%
Time in range greater than 50% is adequate
Time below range of 8% is acceptable
CGM targets are not evidence-based
questions.question: A 25-year-old female with type 1 diabetes using a CGM has her time in range (70-180 mg/dL) at 45% and time below range (less than 70 mg/dL) at 8%. What are the recommended targets?
questions.rationale: The 2019 International Consensus on Time in Range established CGM targets for type 1 and type 2 diabetes: TIR (70-180) greater than 70% (approximately correlates with HbA1c less than 7%), TBR level 1 (54-69) less than 4%, TBR level 2 (less than 54) less than 1%, TAR level 1 (181-250) less than 25%, TAR level 2 (greater than 250) less than 5%, and GMI/estimated HbA1c less than 7%. Each 5% increase in TIR correlates with meaningful clinical benefit. This patient's TBR of 8% indicates significant hypoglycemia exposure requiring urgent regimen adjustment.

options: Inform the patient that the clinical findings require in-person evaluation for patient safety, offer alternative closer providers if possible, and document the clinical rationale
Continue telehealth management to accommodate patient preference
Prescribe treatment based on telehealth assessment alone despite concerning findings
End the telehealth visit and discharge the patient from care
questions.question: An NP is treating a patient in a telehealth visit. During the visit, the NP notices concerning findings that suggest the patient may need an in-person examination. The patient lives 3 hours from the clinic and prefers telehealth only. What is the NP's responsibility?
questions.rationale: The NP's primary obligation is patient safety, which supersedes convenience. When telehealth assessment is insufficient for safe clinical decision-making, the NP must clearly communicate the need for in-person evaluation and document the clinical reasoning. Offering alternative closer providers demonstrates patient-centered care. Prescribing without adequate assessment creates liability. Patient abandonment (ending care without referral) is unethical and potentially illegal.

options: Respect the patient's autonomous decision, document the informed refusal thoroughly, and ensure the patient understands the consequences
Override the patient's decision in their best interest
Discharge the patient from the practice
Involve the ethics committee to override the refusal
questions.question: A patient with decision-making capacity refuses a recommended treatment after receiving thorough information about the diagnosis, treatment options, risks, benefits, and alternatives. The NP disagrees with the patient's decision. What is the ethically appropriate action?
questions.rationale: Patient autonomy is a foundational ethical principle. A competent patient who has received adequate information has the right to refuse any treatment, even life-saving interventions. The NP must respect this decision, document the informed refusal including the discussion of risks and alternatives, and continue to provide care. The ethics committee does not have authority to override a competent patient's decision. Abandonment (discharge without referral) is unethical and potentially illegal.

options: Rifampin significantly reduces oral contraceptive efficacy and alternative contraception is required
Rifampin enhances the contraceptive effect so the dose can be reduced
There is no interaction between rifampin and oral contraceptives
Switching to a progesterone-only pill will prevent the interaction
questions.question: A 38-year-old female taking combined oral contraceptives is prescribed rifampin for latent tuberculosis. What counseling should the nurse practitioner provide?
questions.rationale: Rifampin is one of the most potent CYP3A4 inducers and dramatically accelerates the metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and progestins in oral contraceptives, reducing their efficacy and increasing the risk of unintended pregnancy. Alternative or additional non-hormonal contraception (IUD, condoms) must be used during and for at least one month after rifampin therapy. Progesterone-only pills are equally affected by CYP3A4 induction.

options: Preeclampsia with severe features (HELLP syndrome); initiate magnesium sulfate, antihypertensive therapy, and plan for delivery
Gestational hypertension; monitor blood pressure weekly
Chronic hypertension; adjust oral medications and continue pregnancy
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy; transfer to liver transplant center
questions.question: A 35-year-old G3P2 at 34 weeks gestation presents with blood pressure of 168/108 mmHg, 3+ proteinuria, and platelet count of 88,000/mm3. AST is 245 U/L. She reports epigastric pain. What is the diagnosis and management?
questions.rationale: Severe preeclampsia complicated by HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a life-threatening obstetric emergency. Management includes magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, IV labetalol or hydralazine for severe hypertension, and delivery (the definitive treatment). At 34 weeks with severe features, delivery should not be delayed. Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity may be given if delivery can be safely delayed 48 hours.

options: Aspirin is not generally recommended for primary prevention in adults over 60; for ages 40-59 with 10% or greater ASCVD risk, it is an individualized decision with modest net benefit
Start aspirin 81 mg daily for all adults over 50
Prescribe aspirin 325 mg daily for cardiovascular protection
Aspirin is recommended for all patients with any cardiovascular risk factors
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with a 10-year ASCVD risk of 8.5% asks about aspirin for primary prevention. He has no history of GI bleeding. What does the NP recommend based on current USPSTF guidelines?
questions.rationale: USPSTF (2022) recommends against initiating aspirin for primary CVD prevention in adults 60 and older (D recommendation). For adults 40-59 with 10% or greater 10-year ASCVD risk, the decision is individualized (C recommendation). At 8.5% risk, the net benefit is marginal. The risk of GI bleeding and hemorrhagic stroke must be weighed against potential cardiovascular benefit. Low-dose aspirin (81 mg) is used when indicated.

options: THC is a CB1 receptor partial agonist causing psychoactive effects while CBD has minimal CB1 affinity and is non-intoxicating
Both THC and CBD bind equally to CB1 receptors and cause identical psychoactive effects
CBD is a full agonist at CB1 receptors while THC has no receptor activity
Neither THC nor CBD interact with the endocannabinoid system
questions.question: A patient asks about the mechanism of action of cannabidiol (CBD) compared to tetrahydrocannabinol (THC). Which statement accurately describes the pharmacological difference?
questions.rationale: THC exerts its psychoactive effects primarily through partial agonism at CB1 receptors concentrated in the central nervous system. CBD has very low affinity for CB1 and CB2 receptors and instead modulates the endocannabinoid system indirectly through inhibition of fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH), enhancement of anandamide signaling, and interaction with serotonin 5-HT1A and TRPV1 receptors. This explains why CBD does not produce the intoxication associated with THC.

options: Endometrial biopsy
Reassurance and repeat ultrasound in 6 months
Start progestogen therapy empirically
Hysterectomy
questions.question: A 50-year-old female presents with postmenopausal bleeding for 2 weeks. She is not on hormone therapy. BMI is 38 and she has type 2 diabetes. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 8 mm. What is the next step?
questions.rationale: Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation to rule out endometrial cancer. Endometrial thickness greater than 4 mm on transvaginal ultrasound in a symptomatic postmenopausal woman requires endometrial biopsy. This patient has additional risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, diabetes, postmenopausal status). Reassurance without biopsy risks missing malignancy. Empiric progestogen does not address the need for histologic diagnosis. Hysterectomy without tissue diagnosis is premature.

options: Counsel on seizure precautions, driving restrictions, and offer the option to observe or initiate antiepileptic therapy
Start levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily immediately
Order a repeat EEG in 24 hours and withhold treatment until results
Prescribe phenytoin 300 mg daily and schedule follow-up in 3 months
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with a first unprovoked generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Neurological examination is normal. MRI brain and EEG are normal. She has no family history of epilepsy. What is the recommended management?
questions.rationale: After a first unprovoked seizure with normal neuroimaging and EEG, the risk of recurrence is approximately 30-40%. Current guidelines support either observation with counseling or initiation of AED therapy based on shared decision-making with the patient. Immediate AED initiation without discussion is premature. Phenytoin has significant drug interactions and side effects making it a poor first choice in a young woman of childbearing potential.

options: Standardized medication reconciliation protocol with teach-back method at each transition point and within 72 hours post-discharge
Document the errors and continue current practice
Transfer responsibility to the pharmacist exclusively
Add a medication list to the after-visit summary without verification
questions.question: An NP working in a primary care clinic notices a pattern of medication errors during transitions of care from the hospital to outpatient setting. What quality improvement initiative should the NP implement?
questions.rationale: Medication reconciliation at transitions of care is a Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal. A standardized protocol with teach-back (patient repeats instructions in their own words) reduces errors by ensuring accuracy and patient understanding. The NP should lead the quality improvement initiative. Pharmacist involvement is valuable but responsibility should not be delegated exclusively. Unverified medication lists perpetuate errors.

options: GOLD Stage III - Severe
GOLD Stage II - Moderate
GOLD Stage I - Mild
GOLD Stage IV - Very Severe
questions.question: A 55-year-old patient with a 30-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.58 and FEV1 of 52% predicted post-bronchodilator. How should the NP classify this COPD?
questions.rationale: COPD severity is classified by post-bronchodilator FEV1. GOLD Stage II (Moderate) corresponds to FEV1 50% to less than 80% predicted. An FEV1 of 52% falls within this range. Stage I is FEV1 80% or greater. Stage III is FEV1 30% to less than 50%. Stage IV is FEV1 less than 30% or less than 50% with chronic respiratory failure.

options: ACE inhibitor
Thiazide diuretic
Beta-blocker
Alpha-blocker
questions.question: A 66-year-old female presents with a blood pressure of 162/94 mmHg confirmed on three separate visits. She has stage 3 CKD and microalbuminuria. Fasting glucose is 118 mg/dL. Which antihypertensive class provides the most comprehensive benefit?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors are preferred in patients with CKD, proteinuria/microalbuminuria, and diabetes or prediabetes because they reduce intraglomerular pressure, slow CKD progression, reduce proteinuria, and provide cardiovascular protection. Thiazides are first-line for uncomplicated hypertension but lack renoprotective effects. Beta-blockers are not first-line. Alpha-blockers are associated with orthostatic hypotension and are not preferred.

options: Light therapy using a 10,000-lux light box for 30 minutes each morning within 1 hour of waking
Melatonin 10 mg at bedtime
Tanning bed exposure 3 times weekly
Dark therapy (complete darkness from 6 PM to 8 AM)
questions.question: A 40-year-old female presents with seasonal depression that recurs every winter and remits in spring. PHQ-9 is 12. She has tried sertraline with minimal benefit. What non-pharmacological treatment has the strongest evidence for seasonal affective disorder?
questions.rationale: Bright light therapy is the first-line treatment for seasonal affective disorder (SAD), with efficacy comparable to antidepressants. The recommended protocol is 10,000 lux for 20-30 minutes each morning within the first hour of waking. Light suppresses melatonin and shifts circadian rhythms. Response typically occurs within 1-2 weeks. UV-filtered light boxes are used (NOT tanning beds, which have UV skin cancer risk). Combine with CBT for SAD for the best outcomes. Dawn simulation is an alternative.

options: One-time abdominal ultrasound screening for AAA (USPSTF recommends for men 65-75 who have ever smoked)
CT angiography annually
No screening since he quit smoking
Screening only if he develops symptoms
questions.question: A 60-year-old male asks about abdominal aortic aneurysm screening. He smoked for 25 years but quit 10 years ago. He has no symptoms. What does the NP recommend?
questions.rationale: USPSTF recommends one-time screening for AAA with abdominal ultrasound in men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked. This patient will qualify in 5 years. Smoking history (not current status) determines eligibility. CT angiography is not used for screening due to radiation exposure and cost. Waiting for symptoms (rupture presents with sudden death in 80% of cases) defeats the purpose of screening.

options: Supervised exercise program and cilostazol if exercise alone is insufficient
Immediate vascular surgery referral for bypass
Addition of clopidogrel to aspirin
Compression stockings and leg elevation
questions.question: A 70-year-old male with peripheral arterial disease complains of bilateral calf pain when walking 200 meters that resolves with rest. Ankle-brachial index is 0.65. He currently takes aspirin and atorvastatin. What additional intervention should the NP recommend?
questions.rationale: Intermittent claudication with ABI 0.65 indicates moderate PAD. First-line management includes supervised exercise therapy (35-50 minutes, 3 times weekly), which improves walking distance and quality of life. Cilostazol (phosphodiesterase inhibitor) is added for persistent symptoms. Surgical referral is for critical limb ischemia or lifestyle-limiting symptoms despite conservative management. Compression stockings are for venous disease, not arterial.

options: Start controlled supplemental oxygen targeting SpO2 88-92%, administer systemic corticosteroids and antibiotics
Apply high-flow oxygen at 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask
Intubate and initiate mechanical ventilation
Prescribe oral prednisone and azithromycin for outpatient management
questions.question: A patient with COPD presents with worsening dyspnea, increased sputum production and purulence, and fever. Current SpO2 is 86% on room air. ABG shows pH 7.32, PaCO2 55 mmHg, PaO2 52 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?
questions.rationale: Acute COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and hypoxemia requires controlled oxygen therapy (target 88-92% to avoid suppressing hypoxic respiratory drive), systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg for 5 days), and antibiotics (if increased sputum purulence suggests infection). High-flow oxygen can worsen CO2 retention in COPD patients. Intubation is premature if NIV is available. Outpatient management is inappropriate given the severity.

options: Oral sodium bicarbonate supplementation to maintain serum bicarbonate at 22-26 mEq/L; this slows CKD progression
No treatment needed for CKD-associated acidosis
IV sodium bicarbonate infusion
Acetazolamide to stimulate acid excretion
questions.question: A 60-year-old male with CKD stage 4 on appropriate medications develops metabolic acidosis with bicarbonate of 16 mEq/L. He has no acute illness. What treatment should be initiated?
questions.rationale: Metabolic acidosis in CKD results from impaired acid excretion and reduced ammoniagenesis. KDIGO guidelines recommend treating CKD-associated metabolic acidosis with oral alkali (sodium bicarbonate 650 mg-1300 mg TID or sodium citrate) to maintain serum bicarbonate at 22-26 mEq/L. Benefits include: slowed CKD progression (multiple RCTs show reduced eGFR decline), improved nutritional status (reduces protein catabolism), and improved bone health. Veverimer (non-absorbed acid binder) is a newer option that avoids sodium loading concerns of sodium bicarbonate.

options: TNF inhibitor (adalimumab, infliximab) or IL-17 inhibitor (secukinumab, ixekizumab) as both effectively treat skin and joint disease
Topical calcineurin inhibitor as monotherapy
Oral corticosteroid daily
Cyclosporine indefinitely
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with chronic plaque psoriasis covering 25% BSA has failed methotrexate (hepatotoxicity concerns) and phototherapy (logistical barriers). He has psoriatic arthritis affecting his hands. What biologic class should the NP discuss?
questions.rationale: Moderate-to-severe psoriasis with concomitant psoriatic arthritis failing conventional systemic therapy is an indication for biologic therapy. TNF inhibitors and IL-17 inhibitors have dual efficacy for both skin psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab) are highly effective for skin but have less joint data. Topical therapy alone is insufficient for 25% BSA involvement. Long-term oral corticosteroids risk severe rebound psoriasis upon withdrawal.

options: Secondary hyperparathyroidism from CKD; manage with phosphate binders, active vitamin D (calcitriol), dietary phosphorus restriction, and possibly calcimimetics
Primary hyperparathyroidism requiring parathyroidectomy
Vitamin D toxicity requiring medication discontinuation
Milk-alkali syndrome from excessive calcium intake
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is evaluating a 65-year-old female with a GFR of 22 mL/min (CKD stage 4). Labs show: calcium 8.0 mg/dL, phosphorus 6.2 mg/dL, PTH 320 pg/mL (elevated), and vitamin D 12 ng/mL (low). What is the pathophysiology and management of these findings?
questions.rationale: CKD stage 4 causes secondary hyperparathyroidism through: reduced 1-alpha hydroxylation (low active vitamin D), phosphorus retention (impaired excretion), and hypocalcemia (reduced calcium absorption). The parathyroids compensate by increasing PTH to maintain calcium homeostasis (CKD-MBD: mineral bone disorder). Management per KDIGO: phosphate binders with meals (sevelamer, calcium acetate), calcitriol or paricalcitol (active vitamin D), dietary phosphorus restriction (800-1000 mg/day), and cinacalcet (calcimimetic) if PTH remains elevated. Primary hyperparathyroidism has HIGH calcium. Vitamin D toxicity causes hypercalcemia.

options: Insert a posterior nasal pack or epistaxis balloon and arrange ENT consultation
Apply additional anterior packing and continue observation
Cauterize the nasal septum with silver nitrate
Discharge with instructions to continue pinching the nose at home
questions.question: A patient presents with a nosebleed (epistaxis) that has not stopped despite 20 minutes of direct pressure with anterior nasal packing. Blood is visible in the posterior pharynx. What is the next step?
questions.rationale: Posterior epistaxis (bleeding visible in the posterior pharynx that does not respond to anterior packing) requires posterior nasal packing or a specialized epistaxis balloon to tamponade the bleeding source. ENT consultation is essential as posterior bleeds often require endoscopic cauterization or arterial ligation. Additional anterior packing will not address a posterior source. Silver nitrate cauterization is for anterior bleeds only. Discharging a patient with active posterior epistaxis is unsafe.

options: Triple therapy: PPI, clarithromycin, and amoxicillin for 14 days
PPI monotherapy for 8 weeks
Bismuth quadruple therapy as initial regimen
Empiric trial of H2 receptor antagonist for 4 weeks
questions.question: A 45-year-old male presents with a 6-week history of epigastric burning pain that worsens on an empty stomach and improves with eating. H. pylori stool antigen is positive. He has no alarm symptoms. What is the appropriate first-line treatment?
