questions.rationale: First-line H. pylori eradication in areas with clarithromycin resistance below 15% is PPI-based triple therapy (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin) for 14 days. PPI monotherapy does not eradicate H. pylori and permits reinfection. Bismuth quadruple therapy is an alternative first-line or salvage regimen. H2 blockers do not address the underlying infection.

options: Indeterminate result with 15-30% malignancy risk; molecular testing (Afirma, ThyroSeq) or diagnostic lobectomy is recommended
Benign; no further evaluation needed
Malignant; total thyroidectomy required
Insufficient specimen; repeat FNA in 3 months
questions.question: A 55-year-old male presents with a painless thyroid nodule. FNA cytology returns Bethesda category IV (follicular neoplasm). What does this result indicate?
questions.rationale: Bethesda category IV (follicular neoplasm/suspicious for follicular neoplasm) is an indeterminate cytology with 15-30% malignancy risk. The distinction between follicular adenoma and follicular carcinoma requires evaluation of capsular/vascular invasion, which cannot be determined by FNA. Options include: 1) Molecular testing (ThyroSeq v3, Afirma GSC) to risk-stratify, 2) Diagnostic lobectomy for definitive histological evaluation. If molecular testing suggests benign, surveillance may be appropriate. If malignant features are found on lobectomy, completion thyroidectomy may follow.

options: Combined oral contraceptive pill and metformin with lifestyle modifications
Spironolactone monotherapy
Clomiphene citrate for ovulation induction
Dexamethasone suppression for adrenal hyperandrogenism
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with a BMI of 28 presents with irregular menses, hirsutism, and acne. Labs show elevated free testosterone, normal DHEA-S, and LH-to-FSH ratio of 3:1. Fasting glucose is 6.4 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.rationale: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) with hyperandrogenism, oligomenorrhea, and impaired fasting glucose is managed with combined OCP (suppresses ovarian androgen production, regulates cycles) and metformin (addresses insulin resistance and metabolic risk). Lifestyle modification for weight loss is essential. Spironolactone can be added for persistent hirsutism but is not used as monotherapy initially. Clomiphene is for fertility. Dexamethasone is for congenital adrenal hyperplasia, not PCOS.

options: Offer continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis or post-coital prophylaxis after behavioral modifications have been attempted
Prescribe a 14-day course of IV antibiotics
Order a CT urogram to evaluate for structural abnormalities
Start empiric antifungal therapy for possible resistant organism
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is evaluating a 25-year-old female with recurrent urinary tract infections (4 episodes in the past year). Urine culture consistently grows E. coli. Renal ultrasound is normal. What is the most appropriate management strategy?
questions.rationale: Recurrent UTIs (3 or more episodes/year) in premenopausal women with normal anatomy warrant a stepwise approach: first behavioral modifications (post-coital voiding, adequate hydration, cranberry products), then antibiotic prophylaxis if recurrences continue. Options include continuous low-dose prophylaxis (nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg nightly) or post-coital prophylaxis (single dose after intercourse). IV antibiotics are for complicated UTIs. CT urogram is indicated for suspected structural abnormalities (already ruled out by ultrasound). Antifungals are inappropriate for bacterial UTIs.

options: Pain management with ketorolac and opioids as needed, alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) as medical expulsive therapy, and follow-up imaging in 2-4 weeks
Emergent surgical intervention
Observation without pain management
Lithotripsy immediately
questions.question: A 35-year-old female presents with severe flank pain radiating to the groin. CT KUB shows a 7 mm obstructing ureteral stone at the ureterovesical junction. She has no fever and normal WBC. Creatinine is normal. What is the initial management?
questions.rationale: Uncomplicated ureteral stones less than 10 mm without infection or renal failure can be managed with medical expulsive therapy (MET). Tamsulosin (alpha-1 blocker) relaxes ureteral smooth muscle, facilitating stone passage (NNT approximately 4 for stones 5-10 mm). Pain management with NSAIDs (ketorolac is first-line, superior to opioids for renal colic) plus opioids for breakthrough. Stones at the UVJ have the highest spontaneous passage rate. Follow-up imaging in 2-4 weeks confirms passage. Indications for intervention: infection, bilateral obstruction, renal failure, or intractable symptoms.

options: Polysomnography (sleep study) for diagnosis, followed by continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) as first-line treatment if AHI is 15 or greater
Home pulse oximetry as the definitive diagnostic test
Immediate uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) without sleep study
Modafinil for daytime sleepiness as the primary treatment
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is managing a 55-year-old male with suspected obstructive sleep apnea. He reports excessive daytime somnolence, witnessed apneic episodes, and an Epworth Sleepiness Scale score of 16. BMI is 36. What is the diagnostic gold standard and first-line treatment?
questions.rationale: In-laboratory polysomnography (PSG) is the gold standard for OSA diagnosis, measuring apnea-hypopnea index (AHI), oxygen desaturation, sleep architecture, and cardiac rhythm. Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) is an acceptable alternative for uncomplicated cases. CPAP is first-line treatment for moderate-severe OSA (AHI 15 or greater) or symptomatic mild OSA. CPAP reduces AHI, improves daytime function, and decreases cardiovascular risk. Weight loss is adjunctive. Pulse oximetry alone is insufficient. Surgery is second-line. Modafinil treats residual sleepiness despite CPAP, not primary OSA.

options: Calculate the equianalgesic dose of the new opioid and reduce by 25 to 50 percent for incomplete cross-tolerance
Convert directly to the equianalgesic dose of the new opioid
Double the equianalgesic dose to ensure adequate pain control
Taper the current opioid to zero before starting the new opioid
questions.question: A patient with chronic non-cancer pain has been on morphine equivalent doses of 90 mg per day for 6 months with inadequate pain control. The NP wants to rotate to a different opioid. What is the recommended approach for equianalgesic dose conversion?
questions.rationale: When rotating opioids, the equianalgesic dose of the new opioid should be calculated, then reduced by 25 to 50 percent to account for incomplete cross-tolerance between opioids. This prevents overdose while allowing dose titration based on response. Direct conversion without reduction risks overdose. Doubling the dose is dangerous. Complete discontinuation before starting a new opioid causes unnecessary pain and withdrawal symptoms.

options: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis and nerve conduction studies
CT head without contrast
Serum creatine kinase level
MRI of the lumbar spine with gadolinium
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with a 2-day history of ascending bilateral lower extremity weakness that began in the feet and is now at the knees. She had a respiratory infection 3 weeks ago. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in both legs. What diagnostic test is most important to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia following a recent infection is classic for Guillain-Barre syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy). CSF analysis shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count). Nerve conduction studies confirm demyelination. CT head is normal in GBS. CK is for myopathies. MRI spine may show nerve root enhancement but is not the primary diagnostic tool.

options: These are normal physiologic changes of the dying process and do not indicate suffering
These symptoms indicate the patient is in pain and needs increased analgesics
Emergency intubation should be considered
The patient should be transferred to the ICU for monitoring
questions.question: A family member asks the NP about the dying process for their loved one who is actively dying. The patient has developed Cheyne-Stokes respirations and mottled extremities. What should the NP explain?
questions.rationale: Cheyne-Stokes respirations (cyclical breathing with periods of apnea) and mottled extremities (peripheral circulatory changes) are expected signs of the active dying process. They are not indicators of pain or distress and do not require aggressive medical intervention. Explaining these normal changes to family members reduces anxiety and supports informed decision-making. Increasing analgesics is not indicated without pain assessment. Intubation and ICU transfer are not consistent with comfort-focused care in actively dying patients.

options: Estrogen-only therapy (conjugated equine estrogen or estradiol)
Combined estrogen-progestin therapy
Paroxetine 7.5 mg daily
Black cohosh supplementation
questions.question: A 52-year-old female in menopause presents with moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms significantly impacting quality of life. She underwent hysterectomy 5 years ago for benign disease. She has no history of breast cancer or VTE. What is the recommended first-line therapy?
questions.rationale: In women with prior hysterectomy, estrogen-only therapy is the standard for vasomotor symptoms as progestin is not needed for endometrial protection. Combined therapy carries higher breast cancer risk and is unnecessary after hysterectomy. Paroxetine is an alternative for women with contraindications to HRT. Black cohosh has insufficient evidence for efficacy.

options: Schedule a dental visit by age 1 year or within 6 months of first tooth eruption
Wait until the child has a full set of primary teeth around age 3
Visit the dentist when the child can cooperate with an examination at age 4 to 5
No dental visit needed until permanent teeth begin erupting at age 6
questions.question: A parent asks when their child should first visit a dentist. The child is 11 months old with two erupted lower central incisors. What should the NP recommend?
questions.rationale: The Canadian Dental Association and AAPD recommend the first dental visit by age 1 year or within 6 months of the eruption of the first tooth. Early dental visits establish a dental home, assess risk for caries, provide anticipatory guidance on oral hygiene, and allow for early intervention. Delaying dental care until age 3, 4-5, or 6 misses opportunities for early caries prevention and detection.

options: Moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended for primary prevention when 10-year ASCVD risk is 7.5-20% after clinician-patient risk discussion
Statin therapy is only indicated for ASCVD risk greater than 20%
Lifestyle modifications only; no statin indicated
High-intensity statin therapy regardless of risk discussion
questions.question: A 50-year-old female with a 10-year ASCVD risk score of 12% asks about statin therapy. She has no diabetes, LDL is 145 mg/dL, and she has no history of ASCVD events. According to ACC/AHA guidelines, what is the recommendation?
questions.rationale: ACC/AHA 2018 cholesterol guidelines recommend statin therapy based on four benefit groups: 1) Clinical ASCVD (secondary prevention -- high-intensity statin), 2) LDL 190 or greater (high-intensity statin), 3) Diabetes age 40-75 (moderate-intensity, high if risk factors), 4) Primary prevention with 10-year ASCVD risk 7.5-20% (moderate-intensity after risk discussion). This patient falls in group 4. Risk-enhancing factors (family history, CRP, CAC score, ethnicity-specific risks) can inform shared decision-making. If ASCVD risk is 5-7.5%, consider risk enhancers; if borderline, CAC scoring can guide the decision.

options: Tenofovir/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC) backbone plus an integrase inhibitor (dolutegravir or bictegravir); TDF/FTC also treats hepatitis B
Abacavir/lamivudine plus efavirenz
Zidovudine/lamivudine plus ritonavir-boosted lopinavir
Defer treatment until CD4 falls below 350
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a CD4 count of 450 and viral load of 45,000 copies/mL is newly diagnosed with HIV. He has no opportunistic infections and hepatitis B surface antigen is positive. What is the recommended initial antiretroviral regimen?
questions.rationale: Current guidelines recommend immediate ART initiation for all patients with HIV regardless of CD4 count. In HIV/HBV coinfection, the regimen must include tenofovir (TDF or TAF) and emtricitabine (or lamivudine) as the NRTI backbone because both have HBV activity. Discontinuing these agents risks HBV flare. Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir) are preferred third agents due to high efficacy, tolerability, and high barrier to resistance.

options: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg twice daily for 7 days
TMP-SMX single dose
questions.question: A 22-year-old female presents with dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic pain for 2 days. She is not pregnant and has no history of complicated UTI. Urinalysis shows nitrites and leukocyte esterase. What is the recommended empiric treatment?
questions.rationale: Uncomplicated cystitis in a non-pregnant female is treated with nitrofurantoin (first-line per IDSA guidelines) for 5 days. Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for complicated UTIs due to resistance concerns and side effects. Amoxicillin-clavulanate has inferior cure rates. Single-dose TMP-SMX is less effective than the standard 3-day course.

options: Long-term oxygen therapy, pulmonary rehabilitation, and urgent lung transplant evaluation referral
Increase nintedanib dose beyond maximum
Start high-dose corticosteroids
Switch to inhaled bronchodilators
questions.question: A 70-year-old male with IPF on nintedanib presents with progressive dyspnea (FVC declined from 65% to 50% predicted over 12 months) despite antifibrotic therapy. SpO2 is 85% on room air at rest. What should the NP initiate?
questions.rationale: Progressive IPF despite antifibrotic therapy with significant oxygen desaturation and FVC decline indicates advanced disease. Management: 1) Long-term oxygen therapy for resting SpO2 less than 88% (improves exercise tolerance and quality of life), 2) Pulmonary rehabilitation (improves functional capacity despite unchanged lung function), 3) Lung transplant evaluation (IPF is the most common indication for single lung transplant; transplant should be considered when FVC less than 80% predicted or DLCO less than 40%). IPF is NOT an inflammatory disease -- corticosteroids are ineffective and potentially harmful (PANTHER trial showed increased mortality with prednisone/azathioprine/NAC combination).

options: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (sertraline or escitalopram)
Tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline)
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor (phenelzine)
Benzodiazepine (lorazepam) for 2 weeks
questions.question: A 35-year-old female presents with persistent depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, fatigue, poor concentration, and feelings of worthlessness for 3 months. She has no suicidal ideation and no history of mania. She is not pregnant. What is the first-line pharmacotherapy?
questions.rationale: Major depressive disorder meeting DSM-5 criteria (5 or more symptoms for 2 or more weeks with functional impairment) is first treated with SSRIs due to favorable efficacy, safety profile, and tolerability. Sertraline and escitalopram have strong evidence. TCAs have more side effects and cardiac toxicity risk. MAOIs require dietary restrictions and are reserved for treatment-resistant depression. Benzodiazepines do not treat depression and carry dependence risk.

options: Liraglutide (GLP-1 receptor agonist) because it delays gastric emptying as part of its mechanism of action
Metformin should be stopped first
Glipizide worsens gastric motility
All medications must be continued unchanged
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with longstanding type 2 diabetes develops gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying study. He is on metformin, liraglutide, and glipizide. Which medication should be discontinued because it worsens gastroparesis?
questions.rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) delay gastric emptying as one of their glucose-lowering mechanisms. This effect can exacerbate gastroparesis symptoms (nausea, vomiting, early satiety, abdominal distension). FDA labeling includes gastroparesis as a relative contraindication. In a patient with confirmed gastroparesis, GLP-1 RAs should be discontinued and alternative diabetes medications (SGLT2 inhibitor, DPP-4 inhibitor, insulin) used. Metformin can cause GI side effects but does not affect gastric motility. Prokinetic therapy (metoclopramide) should be initiated for gastroparesis management.

options: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease)
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Goodpasture syndrome
Lupus nephritis
questions.question: A 25-year-old male presents with cola-colored urine and sore throat that began 2 days ago. Urinalysis shows dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts. Serum IgA is elevated. C3 and C4 complement levels are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: IgA nephropathy is the most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide. It characteristically presents with gross hematuria concurrent with or within 1-2 days of an upper respiratory infection (synpharyngitic hematuria), distinguishing it from post-streptococcal GN (which occurs 1-3 weeks after infection). Elevated serum IgA and normal complement levels are typical. Post-streptococcal GN has low C3. Definitive diagnosis requires renal biopsy showing mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence.

options: Process measures (HbA1c testing rates, foot exam completion, eye referral rates) and outcome measures (percentage of patients at HbA1c goal, ED visits for diabetes complications, patient satisfaction)
Only HbA1c levels as the sole metric
Financial metrics exclusively
Patient complaint rates only
questions.question: An NP working in a primary care clinic notices that her patient outcomes for diabetic management are below the national benchmark. She decides to implement a quality improvement project. What metrics should she track?
questions.rationale: Quality improvement in healthcare requires tracking both process measures (did we do the right things?) and outcome measures (did patients get better?). For diabetes management: Process measures include HbA1c testing frequency, annual foot and eye exams, medication reconciliation, and self-management education referrals. Outcome measures include HbA1c control rates (less than 7%, or individualized), hypoglycemia incidence, ED/hospitalization rates, and patient-reported outcomes. Balancing measures ensure improvement in one area does not cause deterioration in another. HEDIS measures provide standardized diabetes quality benchmarks.

options: Teriparatide (anabolic agent) given the fracture and continued glucocorticoid use; or bisphosphonate if teriparatide is not available
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone
Denosumab as first-line without considering anabolic therapy
Raloxifene for its anti-resorptive properties
questions.question: A 60-year-old female on chronic prednisone 15 mg daily for rheumatoid arthritis for 3 years presents with vertebral compression fracture. DEXA T-score is -2.8. What medication should the NP prioritize for glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis?
questions.rationale: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIO) with fracture represents severe disease. ACR guidelines recommend teriparatide (anabolic therapy promoting bone formation) over bisphosphonates for severe GIO, as glucocorticoids primarily suppress bone formation (the mechanism teriparatide counteracts). If teriparatide is unavailable, bisphosphonates are second-line. Calcium/vitamin D alone are insufficient. Raloxifene has limited data in GIO. Glucocorticoid dose should be minimized when possible.

options: Bulimia nervosa; first-line treatment is CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) and fluoxetine (only FDA-approved medication for bulimia)
Anorexia nervosa; nutritional rehabilitation
Binge eating disorder; lisdexamfetamine
ARFID; behavioral feeding therapy
questions.question: A 35-year-old female presents with recurrent episodes of binge eating (consuming large quantities rapidly with loss of control) followed by self-induced vomiting. She binges and purges 3-4 times weekly. She has dental erosion and parotid gland enlargement. BMI is 22. What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?
questions.rationale: Bulimia nervosa is characterized by recurrent binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors (purging, laxative use, excessive exercise) at least once weekly for 3 months. Unlike anorexia, BMI is typically normal or above. Physical findings include dental erosion (perimolysis from gastric acid), parotid hypertrophy, Russell sign (knuckle calluses), metabolic alkalosis, and hypokalemia. First-line treatment: CBT-Enhanced (CBT-E) is the gold standard. Fluoxetine 60 mg/day (higher than typical antidepressant dose) is the only FDA-approved medication. TCAs and bupropion are contraindicated (seizure risk).

options: Cluster headache; high-flow oxygen (12-15 L/min via non-rebreather) and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg
Migraine with aura; oral sumatriptan
Trigeminal neuralgia; carbamazepine
Tension headache; ibuprofen
questions.question: A 40-year-old male presents with severe unilateral periorbital headache with ipsilateral lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, and ptosis. Episodes last 45-90 minutes and occur 2-3 times daily for the past 3 weeks. What is the diagnosis and acute treatment?
questions.rationale: Cluster headache is characterized by severe unilateral periorbital pain with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, miosis, ptosis) lasting 15-180 minutes, occurring in clusters of weeks to months with remission periods. Acute treatment: 100% oxygen at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather (effective in 78% within 15 minutes, no side effects) and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg (fastest onset of any triptan formulation). Oral medications are too slow for cluster attacks. Preventive options: verapamil (first-line), galcanezumab, or lithium.

options: IV alteplase (tPA) within the 4.5-hour window plus consideration for mechanical thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion is identified
Aspirin 325 mg as sole treatment
Wait for MRI before any intervention
IV heparin drip
questions.question: A 75-year-old male presents with acute onset right-sided weakness and aphasia. CT head is negative for hemorrhage. Time of symptom onset is 2 hours ago. NIHSS is 14. What is the treatment?
questions.rationale: Acute ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of symptom onset with significant neurological deficit (NIHSS 14) and no hemorrhage on CT is a clear indication for IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg, 10% bolus then infusion over 60 minutes). CT angiography should be obtained simultaneously to evaluate for large vessel occlusion (LVO), which would qualify for mechanical thrombectomy (endovascular treatment) up to 24 hours using perfusion-based selection criteria. Door-to-needle time target is less than 60 minutes. Aspirin is held for 24 hours after tPA.

options: Hospitalize for medical stabilization, cardiac monitoring, and supervised refeeding with monitoring for refeeding syndrome
Prescribe mirtazapine for appetite stimulation and outpatient follow-up
Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily and refer to a dietitian
Begin cognitive behavioral therapy as outpatient
questions.question: A 19-year-old female presents with a BMI of 16.5, amenorrhea for 6 months, intense fear of weight gain, and distorted body image. She reports restricting food intake to 500 calories daily. Vital signs show HR 48, BP 90/58, temperature 35.8 C. What is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.rationale: This patient has severe anorexia nervosa with medical instability (bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, BMI below 17). Hospitalization is indicated for medical stabilization. Refeeding must be slow and carefully monitored to prevent refeeding syndrome (potentially fatal hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and cardiac arrhythmias). Outpatient management is unsafe at this level of medical compromise. Medications should be deferred until nutritionally stabilized.

options: Administer glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg subcutaneously to reduce secretions
Perform deep oropharyngeal suctioning every 2 hours
Administer IV normal saline to thin secretions
Insert a nasogastric tube for secretion management
questions.question: An NP is managing a patient with end-stage COPD who has a terminal respiratory secretion (death rattle). The patient is unresponsive. What is the most appropriate intervention?
questions.rationale: Glycopyrrolate (an anticholinergic) reduces the production of upper airway secretions that cause the death rattle. It does not cross the blood-brain barrier, making it preferable to scopolamine in terms of central nervous system side effects. Deep suctioning is distressing, often ineffective, and not recommended for terminal secretions. IV fluids can worsen secretion production. Nasogastric tubes are invasive and do not address upper airway secretions.

options: GLP-1 receptor agonist with proven cardiovascular benefit (semaglutide or liraglutide)
Sulfonylurea for cost-effectiveness
DPP-4 inhibitor for weight neutrality
Pioglitazone for insulin sensitization
questions.question: A 65-year-old male with type 2 diabetes on metformin 2000 mg daily has an HbA1c of 7.8%. He has established cardiovascular disease (prior MI). According to ADA 2024 guidelines, what class of medication should be added next?
questions.rationale: ADA Standards of Care recommend that patients with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) receive a GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor with proven CV benefit, independent of baseline HbA1c or metformin use. Liraglutide (LEADER trial) and semaglutide (SUSTAIN-6) demonstrated significant reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). This represents a paradigm shift from glucose-centric to organ-protective prescribing in diabetes management.

options: Refer to reproductive endocrinology for laparoscopic evaluation and fertility assessment
Prescribe combined oral contraceptives for 6 months
Start leuprolide depot injections
Order CA-125 and repeat ultrasound in 3 months
questions.question: A 28-year-old female presents with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. She has been trying to conceive for 14 months without success. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 4 cm left ovarian endometrioma. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.rationale: Endometriosis with infertility and a significant endometrioma warrants referral to a reproductive specialist for laparoscopic evaluation, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Hormonal suppression (OCPs, GnRH agonists) is contraindicated when the patient is actively trying to conceive. CA-125 is nonspecific and does not change management. Watchful waiting delays fertility intervention.

options: Relapse occurs in 50-70% within 1-2 years after methimazole discontinuation; monitor TSH and free T4 every 1-3 months for the first year, then every 6 months
Cure rate is 95% after 18 months; annual TSH only
No monitoring needed after successful treatment course
Lifelong methimazole is required to prevent relapse
questions.question: A 42-year-old female with Graves disease achieved remission on methimazole 18 months ago. TSH is normal. She asks about the likelihood of relapse and monitoring schedule. What should the NP counsel?
questions.rationale: Graves disease remission with thionamide therapy is achieved in only 30-50% of patients, meaning 50-70% relapse, typically within 1-2 years of drug discontinuation. Factors predicting relapse include large goiter, high TRAb levels, male sex, and smoking. Close monitoring with TSH and free T4 every 1-3 months in the first year is essential for early detection. Patients should be counseled about definitive treatment options (RAI or surgery) if relapse occurs.

options: Screen for psoriatic arthritis with CASPAR criteria; order inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) and X-rays of affected joints
Prescribe topical hydrocortisone and follow up in 6 weeks
Order patch testing for contact allergens
Perform KOH preparation for fungal infection
questions.question: A 50-year-old female presents with chronic, symmetric, pruritic plaques on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees. She also reports pitting of her fingernails and dactylitis of the left ring finger. What additional evaluation should the NP perform?
questions.rationale: Plaque psoriasis with nail pitting and dactylitis (sausage digit) strongly suggests concomitant psoriatic arthritis, which occurs in up to 30% of psoriasis patients. Early recognition and treatment prevent irreversible joint damage. CASPAR criteria help classify PsA. Inflammatory markers and imaging establish joint involvement severity. Topical steroids alone miss the systemic joint disease. Contact dermatitis and fungal infection have different presentations.

options: Nephrotic syndrome from diabetic nephropathy; characterized by proteinuria greater than 3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia
Nephritic syndrome with hematuria and red blood cell casts
Acute kidney injury from dehydration
Chronic interstitial nephritis from medication toxicity
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is evaluating a 45-year-old male with a 10-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (A1c 9.2%) who now presents with frothy urine and bilateral pedal edema. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein. 24-hour urine protein is 4.2 g. Serum albumin is 2.8 g/dL. Serum creatinine is 1.8 mg/dL. What clinical syndrome does this represent?
questions.rationale: Nephrotic syndrome is defined by: proteinuria greater than 3.5 g/day (this patient has 4.2 g), hypoalbuminemia (below 3.0 g/dL), peripheral edema (from decreased oncotic pressure), and hyperlipidemia (hepatic overproduction of lipoproteins to compensate for protein loss). Diabetic nephropathy is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults. Management includes: maximum dose ACE inhibitor/ARB, SGLT2 inhibitor, glycemic optimization, sodium restriction, and possibly diuretics for edema. Nephritic syndrome features hematuria, RBC casts, and hypertension. AKI from dehydration would not cause proteinuria of this magnitude.

options: Pituitary apoplexy; immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus, fluid resuscitation, urgent neurosurgical consultation for possible transphenoidal decompression
Subarachnoid hemorrhage; clip or coil aneurysm
Migraine with aura; sumatriptan
Meningitis; empiric antibiotics
questions.question: A 50-year-old male presents with sudden onset of excruciating headache, visual field deficits, and ophthalmoplegia. MRI shows a hemorrhagic pituitary mass. Cortisol is critically low at 1.2 mcg/dL. What is the diagnosis and immediate management?
questions.rationale: Pituitary apoplexy is hemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary adenoma causing sudden expansion, compressing adjacent structures (optic chiasm causing visual field deficits, cavernous sinus causing cranial nerve palsies III/IV/VI). Life-threatening adrenal crisis can occur from acute ACTH deficiency. Immediate management: 1) IV stress-dose hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus, then 50 mg Q8h), 2) Fluid resuscitation, 3) Urgent neurosurgical evaluation for decompression (indicated for progressive visual loss or decreased consciousness). Do NOT delay corticosteroid replacement for confirmatory testing.

options: Combined estrogen plus progestogen therapy (continuous combined or cyclic) to protect the endometrium from unopposed estrogen stimulation
Estrogen-only therapy without progestogen
Testosterone replacement therapy
High-dose progesterone only
questions.question: A 55-year-old postmenopausal female with an intact uterus presents with bothersome hot flashes, night sweats, and vaginal dryness significantly affecting her quality of life. She has no contraindications to hormone therapy. What HRT regimen should the NP prescribe?
questions.rationale: Women with an intact uterus MUST receive progestogen with estrogen to prevent endometrial hyperplasia and cancer (unopposed estrogen increases endometrial cancer risk 5-10 fold). Options: continuous combined (daily estrogen plus progestogen -- eliminates withdrawal bleeding) or cyclic (estrogen daily plus progestogen 12-14 days/month -- causes predictable withdrawal bleeding). Preferred in first 10 years postmenopause or under age 60 (timing hypothesis). Transdermal estradiol has the lowest VTE risk. Women without a uterus can receive estrogen-only therapy.

options: It targets alpha-4-beta-7 integrin selectively in the GI tract, reducing systemic immunosuppression compared to other biologics
It provides systemic immunosuppression equivalent to anti-TNF agents
It has no immunosuppressive properties and works purely through anti-inflammatory mechanisms
It targets all integrin receptors throughout the body equally
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is managing a patient with ulcerative colitis on vedolizumab (Entyvio), a gut-selective integrin inhibitor. What is the advantage of its gut-selective mechanism?
questions.rationale: Vedolizumab selectively binds alpha-4-beta-7 integrin, which is specifically expressed on gut-homing lymphocytes. This gut-selective mechanism reduces lymphocyte trafficking to the intestinal mucosa without broadly suppressing systemic immunity, resulting in a lower risk of opportunistic infections compared to systemic immunosuppressants like anti-TNF agents. It does still carry some infection risk and is immunomodulatory, but the gut selectivity is its distinguishing pharmacological advantage.

options: Postpartum depression with intrusive thoughts (common in PPD); initiate SSRI therapy and arrange psychotherapy, with close follow-up
Postpartum psychosis requiring immediate psychiatric hospitalization
Normal baby blues that will resolve spontaneously
Personality disorder exacerbated by the postpartum period
questions.question: A 32-year-old female presents 6 weeks postpartum with depressed mood, insomnia, poor appetite, difficulty bonding with her infant, intrusive thoughts of harming the baby, and guilt about these thoughts. She reports the thoughts are ego-dystonic and she has no intention of acting on them. What is the diagnosis and appropriate management?
questions.rationale: Postpartum depression occurring beyond 2 weeks postpartum with ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts (the mother is distressed by the thoughts and has no desire to act on them) is a common feature of PPD, not psychosis. Treatment includes SSRI therapy (sertraline is preferred during breastfeeding due to low milk transfer), psychotherapy (CBT or IPT), and close follow-up with safety planning. Postpartum psychosis presents with ego-syntonic delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized behavior. Baby blues resolve by 2 weeks. The 6-week timeline and symptom severity exceed baby blues.

options: Surgical excision with clear margins or Mohs micrographic surgery
Observation and reassess in 6 months
Topical imiquimod 5% cream for 6 weeks
Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen
questions.question: A 55-year-old female presents with a pearly, dome-shaped nodule with visible telangiectasias on the nose. It has slowly enlarged over 1 year and occasionally bleeds. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.rationale: A pearly nodule with telangiectasia on the nose is highly suspicious for nodular basal cell carcinoma. On the nose (high-risk site), Mohs micrographic surgery provides the highest cure rate with tissue-sparing. Standard surgical excision with margin assessment is also appropriate. Observation delays treatment of a malignancy. Topical imiquimod is only for superficial BCC. Cryotherapy does not allow margin assessment and has higher recurrence rates, especially on the nose.

options: Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis; refer for aortic valve replacement (surgical or TAVR)
Moderate AS; monitor annually
Mild AS; lifestyle modifications only
Severe but asymptomatic; defer intervention
questions.question: A 70-year-old male with aortic stenosis has an aortic valve area of 0.8 cm2, mean gradient of 45 mmHg, and LVEF of 60%. He reports dyspnea on exertion. What severity classification and recommended intervention?
questions.rationale: Severe aortic stenosis is defined by aortic valve area less than 1.0 cm2, mean gradient greater than 40 mmHg, and/or peak velocity greater than 4 m/s. This patient has severe AS with symptoms (dyspnea). Symptomatic severe AS has a 2-year mortality of 50% without intervention. Aortic valve replacement (SAVR or TAVR depending on surgical risk and anatomy) is a class I indication. TAVR has become increasingly used across all risk categories. Once symptoms develop (angina, syncope, heart failure), the prognosis deteriorates rapidly without intervention.

options: Topical NSAID or oral acetaminophen
Oral prednisone taper
Intra-articular hyaluronic acid injection
Tramadol
questions.question: An NP is evaluating a 65-year-old female with knee pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Physical examination reveals crepitus, bony enlargement, and limited range of motion. Radiograph shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation. What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment?
questions.rationale: Osteoarthritis management follows a stepwise approach. First-line pharmacologic therapy includes topical NSAIDs (preferred for localized knee OA to minimize systemic effects) or oral acetaminophen. Oral corticosteroids are not recommended for OA management. Intra-articular hyaluronic acid has limited evidence and is not first-line. Tramadol and opioids are reserved for refractory cases due to side effects and dependence risk.

options: In 3 years due to high-risk adenoma features (greater than or equal to 10 mm)
In 10 years as for normal colonoscopy
In 1 year due to piecemeal removal
In 5 years as standard follow-up
questions.question: A 60-year-old male had a screening colonoscopy showing a 2.5 cm sessile polyp in the ascending colon removed by piecemeal polypectomy. Histology shows tubular adenoma with low-grade dysplasia. Margins are clear. When should his next surveillance colonoscopy be performed?
questions.rationale: Per USMSTF 2020 guidelines, high-risk adenomas require 3-year surveillance colonoscopy. High-risk features include: size 10 mm or greater, villous histology, high-grade dysplasia, 3 or more adenomas, or serrated lesions 10 mm or greater. This 25 mm adenoma qualifies based on size alone. Additionally, piecemeal removal of large sessile polyps may warrant earlier surveillance (6-12 months) to assess for residual adenoma at the polypectomy site, followed by the standard interval thereafter.

options: Propranolol or topiramate as first-line migraine prophylaxis given the frequency and disability level
Sumatriptan 100 mg daily as preventive therapy
Opioid analgesics for each headache episode
CT scan of the head before any treatment
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of episodic unilateral pulsating headaches lasting 4-72 hours, accompanied by nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. She has 8-10 headache days per month, significantly impacting her work. What prophylactic treatment should be initiated?
questions.rationale: This patient meets ICHD-3 criteria for migraine without aura. With 8-10 headache days monthly and significant functional impairment, prophylactic therapy is indicated (recommended when there are 4 or more headache days/month or significant disability). First-line prophylactics include beta-blockers (propranolol), anticonvulsants (topiramate, valproate), and antidepressants (amitriptyline). CGRP monoclonal antibodies are newer options. Triptans are abortive, not preventive. Opioids risk medication overuse headache. Neuroimaging is not indicated for classic migraine presentation.

options: She meets criteria for bariatric surgery (BMI 40 or greater, or 35 or greater with obesity-related comorbidities); discuss surgical options, required preoperative evaluation, and lifelong nutritional follow-up
Bariatric surgery is only for BMI greater than 50
Advise another 6-month medically supervised diet before referral
Bariatric surgery does not improve diabetes or hypertension
questions.question: A 35-year-old female with a BMI of 42 and two prior failed medically supervised weight loss attempts asks about bariatric surgery. She has type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and obstructive sleep apnea. What are the NP's counseling points?
questions.rationale: NIH/ASMBS criteria for bariatric surgery include BMI 40 or greater, or BMI 35 or greater with obesity-related comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, OSA). This patient meets criteria. Surgical options include Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, and adjustable gastric banding. Preoperative evaluation includes psychiatric assessment, nutritional counseling, and medical optimization. Benefits include 50-70% excess weight loss, type 2 diabetes remission (60-80% with RYGB), and improved mortality. Lifelong vitamin/mineral supplementation and follow-up are required.

options: Cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil)
Levodopa-carbidopa
Memantine as monotherapy
Haloperidol for behavioral management
questions.question: A 78-year-old male presents with progressive memory loss over 2 years, difficulty with word-finding, and getting lost in familiar places. Mini-Mental State Examination score is 18/30. CT head shows generalized cortical atrophy with hippocampal volume loss. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
questions.rationale: Progressive cognitive decline with hippocampal atrophy and MMSE score consistent with moderate Alzheimer dementia warrants initiation of a cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine) as first-line. Memantine is typically added for moderate-to-severe disease or used when cholinesterase inhibitors are not tolerated. Levodopa is for Parkinson disease. Antipsychotics should only be used for severe behavioral symptoms unresponsive to non-pharmacologic measures.

options: Serum valproic acid level, CBC with platelets, hepatic function tests, and serum ammonia if symptomatic
Serum glucose and HbA1c only
Thyroid function tests and serum calcium only
Renal function and urinalysis only
questions.question: A patient on valproic acid for epilepsy presents for a follow-up visit. Which laboratory tests should the nurse practitioner prioritize?
questions.rationale: Valproic acid requires monitoring of drug levels (therapeutic range 350-700 umol/L), CBC with platelets (risk of thrombocytopenia and pancreatitis), and hepatic function tests (risk of fatal hepatotoxicity, especially in children under 2). Hyperammonemia can occur even with normal liver enzymes and should be checked if the patient presents with confusion, lethargy, or vomiting. Valproic acid does not primarily affect thyroid, renal, or glucose metabolism.

options: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10-14 days
Oral metronidazole 500 mg three times daily for 10 days
IV vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours
Loperamide to control diarrhea and continue clindamycin
questions.question: A 72-year-old nursing home resident develops watery diarrhea (6 episodes per day), abdominal cramping, and low-grade fever after a 10-day course of clindamycin. WBC is 22.4 x 10^9/L. Stool PCR for Clostridioides difficile toxin is positive. What is the first-line treatment?
questions.rationale: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) is treated with oral vancomycin (or fidaxomicin) as first-line per current IDSA guidelines. Oral metronidazole is no longer recommended as first-line due to inferior cure rates. IV vancomycin does not reach adequate intraluminal concentrations. Loperamide is contraindicated as it inhibits toxin clearance and may cause toxic megacolon. The offending antibiotic (clindamycin) should be discontinued if possible.

options: IM ceftriaxone 500 mg single dose plus oral doxycycline 100 mg BID for 14 days plus oral metronidazole 500 mg BID for 14 days
Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose only
IV ampicillin-sulbactam and admission
Wait for NAAT results before initiating treatment
questions.question: A 25-year-old sexually active female presents with purulent vaginal discharge, cervical motion tenderness, and lower abdominal pain. Temperature is 38.1 degrees Celsius. Pregnancy test is negative. NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis are pending. What empiric treatment should be initiated?
questions.rationale: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) requires empiric treatment covering N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis, and anaerobes without waiting for test results. Outpatient treatment for mild-moderate PID includes IM ceftriaxone (for gonorrhea), oral doxycycline (for chlamydia), and oral metronidazole (for anaerobes). Azithromycin alone is insufficient. IV therapy with admission is for severe PID with tubo-ovarian abscess or sepsis. Delaying treatment increases the risk of infertility and chronic pain.

options: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder); physical therapy with gentle stretching exercises and NSAIDs for pain; consider intra-articular corticosteroid injection
Rotator cuff tear; immediate surgical referral
Cervical radiculopathy; MRI of the cervical spine
Acromioclavicular joint arthritis; ultrasound-guided aspiration
questions.question: A 50-year-old female presents with a 3-month history of shoulder pain with difficulty reaching overhead and behind her back. She has limited active and passive range of motion. What is the most likely diagnosis and initial management?
questions.rationale: Adhesive capsulitis presents with progressive loss of both active and passive shoulder ROM (the hallmark distinguishing it from rotator cuff pathology, which typically has normal passive ROM). Initial management includes physical therapy focused on gentle stretching and ROM exercises, NSAIDs, and possibly intra-articular corticosteroid injection for pain relief. Most cases resolve over 12-18 months. Surgical intervention (manipulation under anesthesia) is for refractory cases.

options: Reduce the dose significantly as gabapentin is renally cleared
Continue the current dose as gabapentin is hepatically metabolized
Increase the dose due to reduced absorption in renal disease
Discontinue gabapentin and switch to carbamazepine
questions.question: A 60-year-old female with epilepsy and newly diagnosed chronic kidney disease (eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m2) is taking gabapentin 600 mg three times daily for neuropathic pain. What adjustment should the nurse practitioner make?
questions.rationale: Gabapentin is exclusively renally eliminated and requires significant dose reduction in patients with eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73m2. At this level of renal function, the dose should be reduced to approximately 100-300 mg daily. Continuing the full dose risks accumulation causing sedation, ataxia, and respiratory depression. Gabapentin is not hepatically metabolized. Carbamazepine has significant drug interactions and is not a simple substitution.

options: First-degree relatives should be screened with echocardiography; patient needs serial aortic root imaging as bicuspid aortic valve is associated with aortopathy
No family screening or follow-up needed
Immediate surgical referral for valve replacement
Annual cardiac catheterization
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with no cardiac history is found to have a bicuspid aortic valve on echocardiogram. The valve is mildly thickened but non-stenotic. Aortic root measures 3.8 cm. What screening is recommended?
questions.rationale: Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is the most common congenital cardiac anomaly (1-2% of the population). It is associated with aortopathy (ascending aortic dilation, aneurysm, dissection) independent of valve dysfunction. BAV has autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance -- first-degree relatives should be screened. Serial imaging of the ascending aorta is recommended (echocardiography or CT/MRA). Surgical intervention for the aorta is recommended when it reaches 5.0-5.5 cm or grows rapidly (greater than 0.5 cm/year).

options: Live vaccines are contraindicated, and inactivated vaccine efficacy may be reduced due to B-cell depletion
All vaccines are contraindicated during rituximab therapy
Vaccines are more effective during rituximab therapy due to enhanced immune response
Only live vaccines should be administered during active immunosuppression
questions.question: A patient on rituximab (Rituxan) for rheumatoid arthritis requires influenza vaccination. What vaccine-related consideration is most important?
questions.rationale: Rituximab depletes CD20-positive B cells, which are essential for humoral immune responses. Live vaccines are absolutely contraindicated due to the risk of vaccine-derived infection in immunosuppressed patients. Inactivated vaccines can be given but may have reduced efficacy because B-cell depletion impairs antibody production. Ideally, vaccinations should be completed at least 4 weeks before rituximab initiation or given at least 6 months after the last rituximab dose to maximize vaccine response.

options: HPV vaccination is recommended as a 3-dose series for those starting at age 15 or older
HPV vaccine is no longer effective after age 18
Only a single dose is needed at this age
HPV vaccination is only recommended for males
questions.question: A 21-year-old female college student asks about the HPV vaccine. She has not previously been vaccinated. What should the NP recommend?
questions.rationale: HPV vaccination is recommended for individuals through age 26 who were not adequately vaccinated previously. For those initiating the series at age 15 or older, a 3-dose schedule is recommended (0, 1-2, and 6 months). For those starting before age 15, a 2-dose schedule is sufficient. The vaccine is effective up to age 26 (and may be offered to age 45 with shared decision-making). It is recommended for all genders.

options: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime) plus vancomycin (for MRSA coverage) pending cultures
Amoxicillin-clavulanate alone
Azithromycin monotherapy
Oral levofloxacin and outpatient management
questions.question: A 72-year-old male develops fever, productive cough, and confusion 4 days after hospital admission. Chest X-ray shows new right lower lobe infiltrate. Gram stain of sputum shows gram-negative rods. What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?
questions.rationale: Hospital-acquired pneumonia (onset 48+ hours after admission) requires empiric coverage for both MRSA and gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas. Piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime provides gram-negative coverage, and vancomycin or linezolid covers MRSA. Narrow-spectrum agents are insufficient. Outpatient management is inappropriate for HAP. Monotherapy misses likely pathogens.

options: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT)
Atrial flutter
Ventricular tachycardia
Sinus tachycardia
questions.question: A 40-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of rapid palpitations lasting 30 minutes to 2 hours, terminating spontaneously. ECG during an episode shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 170 bpm with pseudo-R waves in V1 and pseudo-S waves in leads II, III, aVF. What is the most likely arrhythmia?
questions.rationale: Paroxysmal regular narrow complex tachycardia with pseudo-R waves in V1 and pseudo-S waves in inferior leads is classic for typical AVNRT. These pseudo-deflections represent retrograde P waves conducted nearly simultaneously with the QRS through the dual AV nodal pathway circuit. AVNRT is the most common paroxysmal SVT (60% of cases), more common in women. First-line treatment: vagal maneuvers, then adenosine. Definitive treatment: catheter ablation of the slow AV nodal pathway (success rate greater than 95%).

options: Pregabalin or duloxetine
Morphine sustained-release
Topical capsaicin only
Carbamazepine
questions.question: A patient with neuropathic pain from diabetic peripheral neuropathy has tried gabapentin 1800 mg daily with minimal improvement. What is the most appropriate next-line therapy?
questions.rationale: For diabetic peripheral neuropathy refractory to gabapentin, guidelines recommend trial of pregabalin (another alpha-2-delta ligand with better bioavailability) or duloxetine (SNRI with evidence for neuropathic pain). These are first-line alternatives. Opioids are not first-line for neuropathic pain. Topical capsaicin may provide adjunctive relief but is not sufficient as monotherapy. Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia but not for diabetic neuropathy.

options: Hold phenytoin doses until the level falls to the therapeutic range; monitor for worsening toxicity symptoms; adjust the dose or consider alternative AED
Increase the dose because symptoms indicate breakthrough seizures
Administer activated charcoal immediately
Give IV phenytoin to rapidly lower the level
questions.question: A 30-year-old female with a seizure disorder presents with a phenytoin level of 28 mcg/mL (therapeutic 10-20). She has nystagmus and ataxia but is alert. What should the NP do?
questions.rationale: Phenytoin toxicity follows a dose-dependent symptom progression: nystagmus (15-25 mcg/mL), ataxia and dysarthria (25-40 mcg/mL), lethargy (40-50 mcg/mL), and coma/seizures (greater than 50 mcg/mL). At 28 mcg/mL, holding doses and monitoring is appropriate. Phenytoin follows Michaelis-Menten (zero-order) kinetics at therapeutic and supratherapeutic levels, meaning small dose changes cause disproportionately large level changes. This non-linear pharmacokinetics makes phenytoin one of the most challenging medications to dose. Free phenytoin level should be checked if albumin is low.

options: TNF inhibitor (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) or IL-17 inhibitor (secukinumab) as NSAIDs have failed
Methotrexate for axial spondyloarthritis
Oral corticosteroids long-term
Sulfasalazine for spinal disease
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with ankylosing spondylitis on naproxen presents with progressive low back pain and stiffness worse in the morning lasting greater than 1 hour. He has elevated CRP. HLA-B27 is positive. Sacroiliac joint MRI shows active sacroiliitis with bone marrow edema. What treatment should be escalated to?
questions.rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis (axial spondyloarthritis) failing NSAIDs should be escalated directly to biologic therapy. TNF inhibitors are first-line biologics for axial SpA (ATLAS, ASSERT trials demonstrated significant improvement in BASDAI scores, spinal mobility, and MRI inflammation). IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab -- MEASURE trials) are an effective alternative. Important: conventional DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine, leflunomide) are NOT effective for axial (spinal) disease in AS -- they only help peripheral joint involvement. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) are newer options for axial SpA.

options: Confirmatory testing with oral sodium loading test or IV saline infusion test
CT adrenal glands immediately
Start spironolactone empirically
Refer for adrenalectomy
questions.question: A 40-year-old female presents with hypertension resistant to three medications including a diuretic. She has unexplained hypokalemia (K+ 3.1 mEq/L) despite no diuretic-associated potassium wasting. Aldosterone-to-renin ratio is elevated. What is the next diagnostic step?
questions.rationale: An elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio is a screening test for primary aldosteronism and requires confirmatory testing (oral sodium loading, IV saline infusion, or fludrocortisone suppression test) before proceeding to lateralization studies. CT imaging should follow confirmation to identify adenoma vs bilateral hyperplasia. Empiric spironolactone without confirmation is premature. Adrenalectomy requires subtype differentiation first.

options: Meniere disease; initial management includes sodium restriction (less than 2 g/day), diuretic therapy (HCTZ or acetazolamide), and vestibular rehabilitation
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo; Epley maneuver
Vestibular migraine; propranolol
Acoustic neuroma; MRI and surgical excision
questions.question: A 45-year-old female presents with recurrent episodes of intense vertigo lasting 20 minutes to several hours, fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness in the right ear. Audiometry confirms low-frequency hearing loss in the right ear. What is the diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Meniere disease diagnostic criteria: 2 or more episodes of vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours, documented low-to-medium-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear, fluctuating aural symptoms (hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness) in the affected ear. Pathophysiology: endolymphatic hydrops (excess endolymph in the inner ear). Management: 1) Dietary sodium restriction (less than 2 g/day reduces endolymph volume), 2) Diuretics (HCTZ, acetazolamide), 3) Betahistine (used outside US), 4) Intratympanic dexamethasone or gentamicin for refractory cases, 5) Endolymphatic sac surgery for severe refractory disease.

options: Empagliflozin (SGLT2 inhibitor) with patient education about UTI symptoms and hygiene measures
GLP-1 receptor agonist to avoid UTI risk entirely
DPP-4 inhibitor as it has no UTI risk
Sulfonylurea for reliable glucose reduction
questions.question: A 65-year-old female with type 2 diabetes has an HbA1c of 7.2% on metformin 2000 mg daily. She has stage 3a CKD (eGFR 52), heart failure (LVEF 40%), and a history of recurrent UTIs. Which additional agent provides the greatest cardiorenal benefit despite the UTI concern?
questions.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors have shown unprecedented cardiorenal benefits in patients with HFrEF and CKD (EMPEROR-Reduced, DAPA-HF, CREDENCE trials). The cardiovascular and renal benefits significantly outweigh the modest UTI risk. Patient education about genital hygiene, adequate hydration, and prompt reporting of UTI symptoms mitigates this risk. GLP-1 RAs have CV benefit but lack the HF-specific mortality data. DPP-4 inhibitors have neutral CV effects.

options: Bilirubin, INR, and creatinine to determine transplant listing priority
Albumin, ascites grade, and encephalopathy severity
Platelet count, portal vein diameter, and spleen size
AST-to-ALT ratio and alkaline phosphatase
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder presents with ascites, jaundice, and spider angiomata. Labs show albumin 24 g/L, INR 1.8, total bilirubin 68 umol/L, and platelet count 78 x 10^9/L. Abdominal ultrasound confirms cirrhosis with portal hypertension. What is the MELD score component being calculated to guide management priority?
questions.rationale: The MELD (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) score uses serum bilirubin, INR, and creatinine to predict 90-day mortality in cirrhosis and prioritize liver transplant listing. The Child-Pugh score uses albumin, bilirubin, INR, ascites, and encephalopathy. Platelet count and portal vein measurements assess portal hypertension severity but are not MELD components. AST/ALT ratio helps distinguish alcoholic from non-alcoholic liver disease.

options: Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily with careful dose titration
Verapamil 80 mg three times daily
Diltiazem 120 mg extended-release daily
Flecainide 100 mg twice daily
questions.question: A 65-year-old male presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 112 bpm. He has a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 30%). Which rate control agent is most appropriate?
questions.rationale: Beta-blockers such as metoprolol are preferred for rate control in atrial fibrillation with concurrent heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. They should be initiated at low doses and titrated carefully. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are contraindicated in HFrEF because their negative inotropic effects can worsen heart failure. Flecainide is a rhythm control agent contraindicated in structural heart disease due to proarrhythmic risk.

options: Reduce the dose to 1 mg/kg once daily instead of the standard 1 mg/kg every 12 hours for treatment dosing
Use the standard dose of 1 mg/kg every 12 hours without adjustment
Switch to unfractionated heparin which is safer in all renal impairment levels
Double the dose to overcome reduced renal absorption
questions.question: A nurse practitioner is prescribing enoxaparin for a patient with an eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73m2. What dose modification is required?
questions.rationale: Enoxaparin is renally cleared and accumulates in patients with eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73m2, increasing bleeding risk. For treatment-dose enoxaparin, the dose should be reduced to 1 mg/kg once daily instead of every 12 hours. Anti-Xa level monitoring may be considered. While UFH does not require renal adjustment, it is not always necessary to switch; dose-adjusted LMWH can be used with monitoring. Doubling the dose would be dangerous.

options: Infliximab (anti-TNF agent with the strongest evidence for fistulizing Crohn disease)
Vedolizumab
Ustekinumab
Natalizumab
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with Crohn disease develops a perianal fistula despite being on azathioprine. Fistula drainage persists despite antibiotics and seton placement. What biologic therapy is most appropriate?
questions.rationale: Infliximab has the strongest evidence base for fistulizing Crohn disease (ACCENT II trial). It induces and maintains fistula closure. While vedolizumab and ustekinumab are effective for luminal Crohn disease, their evidence for fistula healing is less robust than infliximab. Natalizumab is rarely used due to PML risk. Combined with surgical management (seton, drainage), infliximab provides the best outcomes for perianal fistulas.

options: Bisphosphonate therapy (alendronate) with calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone
Raloxifene as first-line therapy
No treatment until a fracture occurs
questions.question: An NP is evaluating an 80-year-old patient for osteoporosis. DEXA scan shows a T-score of -2.8 at the femoral neck. The patient has no prior fracture history. What is the recommended treatment?
questions.rationale: A T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck or lumbar spine confirms osteoporosis. Bisphosphonate therapy (alendronate, risedronate, or zoledronic acid) is first-line pharmacologic treatment to reduce fracture risk, along with adequate calcium (1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1000 IU/day). Calcium and vitamin D alone are insufficient for osteoporosis treatment. Raloxifene is an alternative but not first-line. Waiting for a fracture exposes the patient to preventable morbidity.

options: Combined estrogen plus progestogen therapy to prevent endometrial hyperplasia in a woman with an intact uterus
Estrogen-only therapy regardless of uterine status
Progestogen-only therapy for vasomotor symptom relief
Testosterone replacement as first-line for menopausal symptoms
questions.question: A 52-year-old postmenopausal female within 10 years of menopause onset presents with severe vasomotor symptoms. She has no contraindications to hormone therapy. What is the recommended regimen?
questions.rationale: In postmenopausal women with an intact uterus, combined estrogen-progestogen therapy is required because unopposed estrogen significantly increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma. The progestogen component protects the endometrium. Estrogen-only therapy is appropriate only for women who have had a hysterectomy. Progestogen alone has limited efficacy for vasomotor symptoms. Testosterone is not first-line for menopausal vasomotor symptoms.

options: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, or oral glucose tolerance test; repeat every 3 years if normal
Random glucose only if symptomatic
Screen only if BMI exceeds 40
No screening until age 50
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with a BMI of 32, blood pressure of 138/88 mmHg, and fasting glucose of 108 mg/dL asks about diabetes screening. He has no symptoms. What is the recommended screening approach?
questions.rationale: USPSTF and ADA recommend screening for type 2 diabetes in adults aged 35-70 with overweight/obesity. This patient has multiple risk factors (BMI 32, borderline hypertension, impaired fasting glucose). Screening can be done with fasting glucose, HbA1c, or OGTT and should be repeated every 3 years if normal. Waiting for symptoms delays diagnosis of a condition that is often asymptomatic in early stages.

options: Oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily with vitamin C
Intramuscular vitamin B12 injection
Folic acid 1 mg daily
Erythropoietin-stimulating agent
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with fatigue and laboratory results showing hemoglobin 98 g/L, MCV 68 fL, ferritin 8 mcg/L, and TIBC 450 mcg/dL. Which treatment should the NP prescribe?
questions.rationale: The laboratory findings of microcytic anemia (low MCV), low ferritin, and elevated TIBC are diagnostic of iron deficiency anemia. Oral iron supplementation with ferrous sulfate is first-line therapy. Vitamin C enhances iron absorption. B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia with high MCV. Folic acid deficiency also causes macrocytic anemia. Erythropoietin-stimulating agents are used in anemia of chronic kidney disease, not iron deficiency.

options: Octreotide subcutaneously to reduce secretions and dexamethasone for anti-inflammatory effect
High-dose metoclopramide for bowel stimulation
Nasogastric tube insertion and bowel rest only
Aggressive IV hydration and total parenteral nutrition
questions.question: A palliative care patient with bowel obstruction from peritoneal carcinomatosis develops nausea, vomiting, and colicky abdominal pain. Surgery is not an option. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.rationale: In malignant bowel obstruction where surgery is not feasible, medical management focuses on symptom control. Octreotide (a somatostatin analog) reduces GI secretions and may help resolve partial obstructions. Dexamethasone has anti-inflammatory and anti-edema effects that can reduce perilesional swelling. Metoclopramide (a prokinetic) is contraindicated in complete bowel obstruction as it can worsen colicky pain and perforation risk. NG tubes are uncomfortable and a last resort. Aggressive hydration may worsen edema and secretions.

options: Tyramine in aged cheese was not metabolized due to MAO-A inhibition, causing a catecholamine surge
The MAOI caused direct vasoconstriction from cheese protein binding
Cheese increased MAOI absorption leading to drug toxicity
An allergic reaction to the cheese triggered histamine release
questions.question: A 35-year-old male on an MAOI (phenelzine) for treatment-resistant depression reports eating aged cheese and developing a severe headache with blood pressure of 210/120 mmHg. What is the cause of this hypertensive crisis?
questions.rationale: MAOIs inhibit monoamine oxidase-A, which normally metabolizes tyramine in the gut. When tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats, fermented foods) are consumed, unmetabolized tyramine enters the systemic circulation and causes massive norepinephrine release from sympathetic nerve terminals, resulting in hypertensive crisis. Treatment includes phentolamine or nifedipine. Patients on MAOIs must follow strict dietary restrictions. This is a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic interaction, not an allergy.

options: Obtain blood cultures and initiate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
Administer acetaminophen and recheck temperature in 4 hours
Hold chemotherapy and schedule outpatient follow-up
Order a chest radiograph and urinalysis before initiating treatment
questions.question: A patient receiving chemotherapy presents with a temperature of 38.5 degrees Celsius and an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 0.4 x 10^9/L. What is the priority action?
questions.rationale: Febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.3 degrees Celsius or higher with ANC less than 0.5 x 10^9/L) is an oncologic emergency. Blood cultures should be obtained and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated within 60 minutes. Delaying antibiotics to obtain imaging increases mortality. Acetaminophen alone does not address the underlying infection risk. Outpatient management may be considered only for low-risk patients meeting specific criteria.

options: Structured weight loss program targeting 7-10% body weight reduction with lifestyle modifications
Start ursodeoxycholic acid for liver protection
Liver biopsy immediately to stage fibrosis
Prescribe metformin specifically for liver disease
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with metabolic syndrome (BMI 32, fasting glucose 6.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 3.2 mmol/L) has elevated ALT at 78 U/L and AST at 52 U/L. Hepatitis B and C serologies are negative. Alcohol intake is minimal. Abdominal ultrasound shows hepatic steatosis. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.rationale: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MASLD) with metabolic syndrome is primarily managed with lifestyle modifications targeting 7-10% weight loss, which has been shown to improve hepatic steatosis, inflammation, and even fibrosis. Ursodeoxycholic acid has no proven benefit in NAFLD. Liver biopsy is reserved for patients with high fibrosis risk scores (FIB-4, NAFLD fibrosis score). Metformin improves insulin resistance but does not specifically improve liver histology.

5-alpha reductase inhibitors such as finasteride

questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a history of acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with urinary retention due to benign prostatic hyperplasia. Which medication class is contraindicated for his urinary symptoms?
questions.rationale: Anticholinergic medications are contraindicated in acute angle-closure glaucoma because they can cause mydriasis and further narrow the anterior chamber angle, precipitating an acute glaucoma attack. Additionally, anticholinergics can worsen urinary retention. Alpha-1 blockers and 5-alpha reductase inhibitors are appropriate treatments for BPH. Tadalafil 5 mg daily is approved for both BPH and erectile dysfunction.

Prescribe topical antibiotic drops and follow up in 1 week

questions.question: A patient presents with a foreign body sensation in the right eye after grinding metal. Visual acuity is 20/40 in the affected eye. Slit lamp examination reveals a metallic foreign body embedded in the cornea with a surrounding rust ring. What is the appropriate management?
questions.rationale: Corneal metallic foreign bodies should be removed promptly to prevent further corneal damage and infection. Removal can be performed under slit lamp guidance using a sterile needle or spud. The rust ring may require removal by ophthalmology with a rotating burr if it does not resolve. Topical antibiotics prevent secondary infection. Simple irrigation is insufficient for an embedded foreign body. Eye patching is no longer routinely recommended for corneal injuries. Emergency surgical removal is not typically needed for uncomplicated corneal foreign bodies.

options: Assess the client's chest pain characteristics and vital signs
Administer aspirin 160 mg chewed as ordered
Obtain a 12-lead ECG
Establish IV access and draw cardiac biomarkers
Administer nitroglycerin sublingually as ordered for persistent pain

questions.question: A client is experiencing an acute STEMI. Place the nursing interventions in the correct order of implementation.
questions.rationale: STEMI management follows a systematic approach: initial assessment (ABCs, pain, vitals), immediate ECG (door-to-ECG under 10 minutes), aspirin administration (antiplatelet), IV access with blood draws, nitroglycerin for pain relief, and preparation for definitive reperfusion (PCI). The ECG confirms the diagnosis and drives the reperfusion decision.

questions.question: A client with atrial fibrillation is on warfarin therapy. Which client statements indicate correct understanding of the medication? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Regular INR monitoring, consistent vitamin K intake, using an electric razor, wearing a medical alert bracelet, and reporting bleeding signs all demonstrate correct warfarin understanding. Taking aspirin with warfarin significantly increases bleeding risk and should not be done without provider approval.

questions.question: A 64-year-old client newly diagnosed with COPD is learning to use a dry powder inhaler (DPI). BP 128/76, HR 74, RR 18, SpO2 94% on room air. Which instruction should the nurse provide?
questions.rationale: Dry powder inhalers require a quick, deep inhalation to generate enough airflow to disperse the powder into fine particles that reach the lower airways. DPIs should not be shaken, exhaled into (moisture clumps the powder), or used with spacers. Slow inhalation is used with metered-dose inhalers, not DPIs.

Assess neurological status for signs of intracranial bleeding
questions.question: A client is receiving a heparin infusion. Which nursing assessments are essential during heparin therapy? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Heparin monitoring includes aPTT (not INR, which monitors warfarin), bleeding assessment at all sites, platelet counts for HIT detection, and neurological assessment for intracranial hemorrhage. INR monitors warfarin's effect on vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, not heparin's effect on antithrombin III.

questions.question: A practical nurse is performing hand hygiene before inserting a urinary catheter. The nurse should scrub hands for a minimum of how many seconds?
questions.rationale: The recommended minimum hand hygiene duration for routine handwashing is 20 seconds with soap and water, which allows adequate friction to remove transient microorganisms. Five or ten seconds is insufficient for effective microbial removal. While 60 seconds is not harmful, it exceeds the recommended minimum and is not required for routine handwashing.

Remove the non-rebreather mask since it is not working properly

questions.question: A 60-year-old client is receiving oxygen via a non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min. BP 100/62, HR 108, RR 32, SpO2 88%. The nurse notices the reservoir bag is completely deflating during each inhalation. Which action should the nurse take?
questions.rationale: The reservoir bag on a non-rebreather mask must remain at least partially inflated (one-third full) during inspiration to ensure the client is receiving high-concentration oxygen and not rebreathing CO2. Complete deflation means the client is not getting adequate oxygen from the reservoir. The flow rate should be increased to keep the bag inflated, or the mask may need adjustment.

Advise wearing high-heeled shoes for better posture
Recommend avoiding all physical activity
questions.question: A nurse is educating a family about caring for their elderly parent with osteoporosis. Which instruction is most important for fracture prevention?
questions.rationale: Environmental modifications such as removing throw rugs, ensuring adequate lighting, and installing grab bars are essential for fall prevention in clients with osteoporosis. Falls are the leading cause of fractures in this population. Bed rest worsens bone loss and muscle weakness. High-heeled shoes increase fall risk. Weight-bearing physical activity actually helps maintain bone density.

My child will need to take anti-seizure medication daily from now on
Febrile seizures always cause brain damage
I should place a spoon in my child's mouth during a seizure to prevent tongue biting
questions.question: A nurse is educating parents about febrile seizures in a 2-year-old child who experienced one during an ear infection. Which statement by the parent indicates correct understanding?
questions.rationale: Simple febrile seizures are common in children aged 6 months to 5 years, are generally benign, and do not indicate epilepsy. Daily anti-seizure medication is not typically prescribed for simple febrile seizures. They do not cause brain damage. Nothing should be placed in the mouth during a seizure as this can cause injury to the child and caregiver.

questions.question: A client with schizophrenia tells the nurse, 'The CIA implanted a tracking device in my brain.' Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?
questions.rationale: The therapeutic response acknowledges the client's experience without reinforcing the delusion. Do not argue with delusions (increases anxiety and entrenchment), do not validate them as real, and do not provide logical arguments against them. The nurse should present reality gently, focus on the feelings behind the delusion, and redirect to reality-based topics. Building trust is essential for therapeutic relationship.

options: It is understandable to feel worried. Cardiac rehabilitation is supervised by trained professionals who monitor your heart during exercise to ensure safety, and the program is tailored to your abilities
You do not need to worry because heart attacks are not serious
Cardiac rehabilitation is optional and most clients skip it

questions.question: A 63-year-old client with a recent STEMI asks about cardiac rehabilitation. The client is worried about exercising after a heart attack. Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?
questions.rationale: Acknowledging the client's fear and providing reassurance about the supervised nature of cardiac rehabilitation reduces anxiety and promotes adherence. Cardiac rehabilitation is evidence-based and reduces mortality by 20 to 30%. Minimizing the MI severity is dismissive. Cardiac rehab is strongly recommended, not optional. A year of inactivity is excessive and harmful.

questions.question: A client with asthma is being taught to use a peak flow meter. Identify the correct position for the indicator on the peak flow meter before each use.
questions.rationale: Before each peak flow attempt, the indicator must be at the baseline (zero) position. If not reset, the indicator will show the previous reading rather than the current one, resulting in an inaccurate measurement. The client should check and reset the indicator before each of the three attempts, recording the best of the three.

questions.question: A nurse is conducting a health screening at a community fair. Which screening test is recommended annually for adults aged 50 and older?
questions.rationale: Colorectal cancer screening is recommended beginning at age 45-50, with various methods including annual fecal occult blood testing or fecal immunochemical testing, or colonoscopy every 10 years. Bone density scans are recommended for women at age 65 and men at age 70, or earlier with risk factors. Cardiac catheterization is a diagnostic procedure, not a screening test. Routine chest X-rays are not recommended for asymptomatic adults.

The temperature of 37.4, as it indicates sepsis
The SpO2 of 96%, as it requires supplemental oxygen
